rows.english: Herein, there is gladdening in two ways, through concentration and through insight.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ samādhivasena?
rows.english: How through concentration?

rows.pali: Sappītike dve jhāne samāpajjati.
rows.english: He attains the two jhānas in which happiness is present.

rows.pali: So samāpattikkhaṇe sampayuttapītiyā cittaṃ āmodeti pamodeti.
rows.english: At the time when he has actually entered upon them he inspires the mind with gladness, instils gladness into it, by means of the happiness associated with the jhāna.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ vipassanāvasena?
rows.english: How through insight?

rows.pali: Sappītike dve jhāne samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhāya jhānasampayuttapītiṃ khayato vayato sammasati.
rows.english: After entering upon and emerging from one of the two jhānas accompanied by happiness, he comprehends with insight that happiness associated with the jhāna as liable to destruction and to fall;

rows.pali: Evaṃ vipassanākkhaṇe jhānasampayuttaṃ pītiṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā cittaṃ āmodeti pamodeti.
rows.english: thus at the actual time of insight he inspires the mind with gladness, instils gladness into it, by making the happiness associated with the jhāna the object.

rows.pali: Evaṃ paṭipanno "abhippamodayaṃ cittaṃ assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti vuccati.
rows.english: It is of one progressing in this way that the words, “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out gladdening the [manner of] consciousness,’” are said.

rows.pali: Samādahaṃ cittanti paṭhamajjhānādivasena ārammaṇe cittaṃ samaṃ ādahanto samaṃ ṭhapento.
rows.english: 232.(xi) Concentrating (samādahaṃ) the [manner of] consciousness: evenly (samaṃ) placing (ādahanto) the mind, evenly putting it on its object by means of the first jhāna and so on.

rows.pali: Tāni vā pana jhānāni samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhāya jhānasampayuttaṃ cittaṃ khayato vayato sampassato vipassanākkhaṇe lakkhaṇapaṭivedhena uppajjati khaṇikacittekaggatā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, when, having entered upon those jhānas and emerged from them, he comprehends with insight the consciousness associated with the jhāna as liable to destruction and to fall, then at the actual time of insight momentary unification of the mind64 arises through the penetration of the characteristics [of impermanence, and so on].

rows.pali: Evaṃ uppannāya khaṇikacittekaggatāya vasenapi ārammaṇe cittaṃ samaṃ ādahanto samaṃ ṭhapento "samādahaṃ cittaṃ assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti vuccati.
rows.english: Thus the words, “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out concentrating the [manner of] consciousness,’” are said also of one who evenly places the mind, evenly puts it on its object by means of the momentary unification of the mind arisen thus.

rows.pali: Vimocayaṃ cittanti paṭhamajjhānena nīvaraṇehi cittaṃ mocento vimocento, dutiyena vitakkavicārehi, tatiyena pītiyā, catutthena sukhadukkhehi cittaṃ mocento vimocento.
rows.english: 233.(xii) Liberating the [manner of] consciousness: he both breathes in and breathes out delivering, liberating, the mind from the hindrances by means of the first jhāna, from applied and sustained thought by means of the second, from happiness by means of the third, from pleasure and pain by means of the fourth.

rows.pali: Tāni vā pana jhānāni samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhāya jhānasampayuttaṃ cittaṃ khayato vayato sammasati.
rows.english: Or alternatively, when, having entered upon those jhānas and emerged from them, he comprehends with insight the consciousness associated with the jhāna as liable to destruction and to fall,

rows.pali: So vipassanākkhaṇe aniccānupassanāya niccasaññāto cittaṃ mocento, dukkhānupassanāya sukhasaññāto, anattānupassanāya attasaññāto, nibbidānupassanāya nandito, virāgānupassanāya rāgato, nirodhānupassanāya samudayato, paṭinissaggānupassanāya ādānato cittaṃ mocento assasati ceva passasati ca.
rows.english: then at the actual time of insight he delivers, liberates, the mind from the perception of permanence by means of the contemplation of impermanence, from the perception of pleasure by means of the contemplation of pain, from the perception of self by means of the contemplation of not self, from delight by means of the contemplation of dispassion, from greed by means of the contemplation of fading away, from arousing by means of the contemplation of cessation, from grasping by means of the contemplation of relinquishment.

rows.pali: Tena vuccati "vimocayaṃ cittaṃ assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti.
rows.english: Hence it is said: [290] “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out liberating the [manner of] consciousness.65 ’”

rows.pali: Evaṃ cittānupassanāvasena idaṃ catukkaṃ bhāsitanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So this tetrad should be understood to deal with contemplation of mind.

rows.pali: 236.Catutthacatukke pana aniccānupassīti ettha tāva aniccaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 234.(xiii) But in the fourth tetrad, as to contemplating impermanence, here firstly, the impermanent should be understood,

rows.pali: Aniccatā veditabbā.
rows.english: and impermanence,

rows.pali: Aniccānupassanā veditabbā.
rows.english: and the contemplation of impermanence,

rows.pali: Aniccānupassī veditabbo.
rows.english: and one contemplating impermanence.

rows.pali: Tattha aniccanti pañcakkhandhā.
rows.english: Herein, the five aggregates are the impermanent.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Uppādavayaññathattabhāvā.
rows.english: Because their essence is rise and fall and change.

rows.pali: Aniccatāti tesaṃyeva uppādavayaññathattaṃ, hutvā abhāvo vā, nibbattānaṃ tenevākārena aṭṭhatvā khaṇabhaṅgena bhedoti attho.
rows.english: Impermanence is the rise and fall and change in those same aggregates, or it is their non-existence after having been; the meaning is, it is the breakup of produced aggregates through their momentary dissolution since they do not remain in the same mode.

rows.pali: Aniccānupassanāti tassā aniccatāya vasena rūpādīsu aniccanti anupassanā.
rows.english: Contemplation of impermanence is contemplation of materiality, etc., as “impermanent” in virtue of that impermanence.

rows.pali: Aniccānupassīti tāya anupassanāya samannāgato.
rows.english: One contemplating impermanence possesses that contemplation.

rows.pali: Tasmā evaṃbhūto assasanto passasanto ca idha "aniccānupassī assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti veditabbo.
rows.english: So it is when one such as this is breathing in and breathing out that it can be understood of him: “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out contemplating impermanence. ’”66

rows.pali: Virāgānupassīti ettha pana dve virāgā khayavirāgo ca accantavirāgo ca.
rows.english: 235.(xiv) Contemplating fading away: there are two kinds of fading away, that is, fading away as destruction, and absolute fading away.67

rows.pali: Tattha khayavirāgoti saṅkhārānaṃ khaṇabhaṅgo.
rows.english: Herein, “fading away as destruction” is the momentary dissolution of formations.

rows.pali: Accantavirāgoti nibbānaṃ.
rows.english: “Absolute fading away” is Nibbāna.

rows.pali: Virāgānupassanāti tadubhayadassanavasena pavattā vipassanā ca maggo ca.
rows.english: Contemplation of fading away is insight and it is the path, which occurs as the seeing of these two.

rows.pali: Tāya duvidhāyapi anupassanāya samannāgato hutvā assasanto passasanto ca "virāgānupassī assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti veditabbo.
rows.english: It is when he possesses this twofold contemplation that it can be understood of him: “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out contemplating fading away. ’”

rows.pali: Nirodhānupassīpadepi eseva nayo.
rows.english: (xv) The same method of explanation applies to the clause, contemplating cessation.

rows.pali: Paṭinissaggānupassīti etthāpi dve paṭinissaggā pariccāgapaṭinissaggo ca pakkhandanapaṭinissaggo ca.
rows.english: 236.(xvi) Contemplating relinquishment: relinquishment is of two kinds too, that is to say, relinquishment as giving up, and relinquishment as entering into.

rows.pali: Paṭinissaggoyeva anupassanā paṭinissaggānupassanā.
rows.english: Relinquishment itself as [a way of] contemplation is “contemplation of relinquishment.”

rows.pali: Vipassanāmaggānaṃ etamadhivacanaṃ.

Vipassanā hi tadaṅgavasena saddhiṃ khandhābhisaṅkhārehi kilese pariccajati, saṅkhatadosadassanena ca tabbiparīte nibbāne tanninnatāya pakkhandatīti pariccāgapaṭinissaggo ceva pakkhandanapaṭinissaggoti ca vuccati.
rows.english: For insight is called both “relinquishment as giving up” and “relinquishment as entering into” since [firstly], through substitution of opposite qualities, it gives up defilements with their aggregate-producing kamma formations, and [secondly], through seeing the wretchedness of what is formed, it also enters into Nibbāna by inclining towards Nibbāna, which is the opposite of the formed (XI.18).

rows.pali: Maggo samucchedavasena saddhiṃ khandhābhisaṅkhārehi kilese pariccajati, ārammaṇakaraṇena ca nibbāne pakkhandatīti pariccāgapaṭinissaggo ceva pakkhandanapaṭinissaggoti ca vuccati.
rows.english: Also the path is called both “relinquishment as giving up” and “relinquishment as entering into” since it gives up defilements with their aggregate-producing kamma-formations by cutting them off, and it enters into Nibbāna by making it its object.

rows.pali: Ubhayampi pana purimapurimaññāṇānaṃ anuanupassanato anupassanāti vuccati.
rows.english: Also both [insight and path knowledge] are called contemplation (anupassanā) because of their re-seeing successively (anu anu passanā) each preceding kind of knowledge.68 [291]

rows.pali: Tāya duvidhāyapi paṭinissaggānupassanāya samannāgato hutvā assasanto passasanto ca "paṭinissaggānupassī assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti veditabbo.
rows.english: It is when he possesses this twofold contemplation that it can be understood of him: “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out contemplating relinquishment. ’”

rows.pali: Idaṃ catutthacatukkaṃ suddhavipassanāvaseneva vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 237. This tetrad deals only with pure insight

rows.pali: Purimāni pana tīṇi samathavipassanāvasena.
rows.english: while the previous three deal with serenity and insight.

rows.pali: Evaṃ catunnaṃ catukkānaṃ vasena soḷasavatthukāya ānāpānassatiyā bhāvanā veditabbā.
rows.english: This is how the development of mindfulness of breathing with its sixteen bases in four tetrads should be understood.

rows.pali: Evaṃ soḷasavatthuvasena ca pana ayaṃ ānāpānassati mahapphalā hoti mahānisaṃsā.
rows.english: This mindfulness of breathing with its sixteen bases thus is of great fruit, of great benefit.

rows.pali: 237.Tatrassa "ayampi kho, bhikkhave, ānāpānassatisamādhi bhāvito bahulīkato santo ceva paṇīto cā"tiādivacanato santabhāvādivasenāpi mahānisaṃsatā veditabbā, vitakkupacchedasamatthatāyapi.
rows.english: 238. Its great beneficialness should be understood here as peacefulness both because of the words, “And, bhikkhus, this concentration through mindfulness of breathing, when developed and much practiced, is both peaceful and sublime” (S V 321), etc., and because of its ability to cut off applied thoughts;

rows.pali: Ayañhi santapaṇītaasecanakasukhavihārattā samādhiantarāyakarānaṃ vitakkānaṃ vasena ito cito ca cittassa vidhāvanaṃ vicchinditvā ānāpānārammaṇābhimukhameva cittaṃ karoti.
rows.english: for it is because it is peaceful, sublime, and an unadulterated blissful abiding that it cuts off the mind’s running hither and thither with applied thoughts obstructive to concentration, and keeps the mind only on the breaths as object.

rows.pali: Teneva vuttaṃ "ānāpānassati bhāvetabbā vitakkupacchedāyā"ti (a. ni. 9.1).
rows.english: Hence it is said: “Mindfulness of breathing should be developed in order to cut off applied thoughts” (A IV 353).

rows.pali: Vijjāvimuttipāripūriyā mūlabhāvenāpi cassā mahānisaṃsatā veditabbā.
rows.english: 239. Also its great beneficialness should be understood as the root condition for the perfecting of clear vision and deliverance;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā – "ānāpānassati, bhikkhave, bhāvitā bahulīkatā cattāro satipaṭṭhāne paripūreti, cattāro satipaṭṭhānā bhāvitā bahulīkatā satta bojjhaṅge paripūrenti, satta bojjhaṅgā bhāvitā bahulīkatā vijjāvimuttiṃ paripūrentī"ti (ma. ni. 3.147).
rows.english: for this has been said by the Blessed One: “Bhikkhus, mindfulness of breathing, when developed and much practiced, perfects the four foundations of mindfulness. The four foundations of mindfulness, when developed and much practiced, perfect the seven enlightenment factors. The seven enlightenment factors, when developed and much practiced, perfect clear vision and deliverance” (M III 82).

rows.pali: Apica carimakānaṃ assāsapassāsānaṃ viditabhāvakaraṇatopissā mahānisaṃsatā veditabbā.
rows.english: 240. Again its great beneficialness should be understood to reside in the fact that it causes the final in-breaths and out-breaths to be known;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā – "evaṃ bhāvitāya kho, rāhula, ānāpānassatiyā evaṃ bahulīkatāya yepi te carimakā assāsapassāsā, tepi viditāva nirujjhanti, no aviditā"ti (ma. ni. 2.121).
rows.english: for this is said by the Blessed One: “Rāhula, when mindfulness of breathing is thus developed, thus practiced much, the final in-breaths and out-breaths, too, are known as they cease, not unknown” (M I 425f.).

rows.pali: 238.Tattha nirodhavasena tayo carimakā bhavacarimakā, jhānacarimakā, cuticarimakāti.
rows.english: 241. Herein, there are three kinds of [breaths that are] final because of cessation, that is to say, final in becoming, final in jhāna, and final in death.

rows.pali: Bhavesu hi kāmabhave assāsapassāsā pavattanti, rūpārūpabhavesu nappavattanti, tasmā te bhavacarimakā.
rows.english: For, among the various kinds of becoming (existence), in-breaths and out-breaths occur in the sensual-sphere becoming, not in the fine-material and immaterial kinds of becoming. That is why there are final ones in becoming.

rows.pali: Jhānesu purime jhānattaye pavattanti, catutthe nappavattanti, tasmā te jhānacarimakā.
rows.english: In the jhānas they occur in the first three but not in the fourth. That is why there are final ones in jhāna.

rows.pali: Ye pana cuticittassa purato soḷasamena cittena saddhiṃ uppajjitvā cuticittena saha nirujjhanti, ime cuticarimakā nāma.
rows.english: Those that arise along with the sixteenth consciousness preceding the death consciousness [292] cease together with the death consciousness. They are called “final in death.”

rows.pali: Ime idha "carimakā"ti adhippetā.
rows.english: It is these last that are meant here by “final. ”

rows.pali: Imaṃ kira kammaṭṭhānaṃ anuyuttassa bhikkhuno ānāpānārammaṇassa suṭṭhu pariggahitattā cuticittassa purato soḷasamassa cittassa uppādakkhaṇe uppādaṃ āvajjayato uppādopi nesaṃ pākaṭo hoti.
rows.english: 242. When a bhikkhu has devoted himself to this meditation subject, it seems, if he adverts, at the moment of arising of the sixteenth consciousness before the death consciousness, to their arising, then their arising is evident to him; (and it is so because he has thoroughly discerned in-breaths and out-breaths as object.)

rows.pali: Ṭhitiṃ āvajjayato ṭhitipi nesaṃ pākaṭā hoti.
rows.english: if he adverts to their presence, then their presence is evident to him;

rows.pali: Bhaṅgaṃ āvajjayato ca bhaṅgo nesaṃ pākaṭo hoti.
rows.english: if he adverts to their dissolution, then their dissolution is evident to him;

rows.pali: Ito aññaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ bhāvetvā arahattaṃ pattassa bhikkhuno hi āyuantaraṃ paricchinnaṃ vā hoti aparicchinnaṃ vā.
rows.english: 243. When a bhikkhu has attained Arahantship by developing some other meditation subject than this one, he may be able to define his life term or not.

rows.pali: Idaṃ pana soḷasavatthukaṃ ānāpānassatiṃ bhāvetvā arahattaṃ pattassa āyuantaraṃ paricchinnameva hoti.
rows.english: But when he has reached Arahantship by developing this mindfulness of breathing with its sixteen bases, he can always define his life term.

rows.pali: So "ettakaṃ dāni me āyusaṅkhārā pavattissanti, na ito para"nti ñatvā attano dhammatāya eva sarīrapaṭijaggananivāsanapārupanādīni sabbakiccāni katvā akkhīni nimīleti koṭapabbatavihāravāsītissatthero viya mahākarañjiyavihāravāsīmahātissatthero viya devaputtamahāraṭṭhe piṇḍapātikatissatthero viya cittalapabbatavihāravāsino dve bhātiyattherā viya ca.
rows.english: He knows, “My vital formations will continue now for so long and no more. ” Automatically he performs all the functions of attending to the body, dressing and robing, etc., after which he closes his eyes, like the Elder Tissa who lived at the Koṭapabbata Monastery, like the Elder Mahā Tissa who lived at the Mahā Karañjiya Monastery, like the Elder Tissa the alms-food eater in the kingdom of Devaputta, like the elders who were brothers and lived at the Cittalapabbata monastery.

rows.pali: Tatridaṃ ekavatthuparidīpanaṃ.
rows.english: 244. Here is one story as an illustration.

rows.pali: Dvebhātiyattherānaṃ kireko puṇṇamuposathadivase pātimokkhaṃ osāretvā bhikkhusaṅghaparivuto attano vasanaṭṭhānaṃ gantvā caṅkame ṭhito candālokaṃ oloketvā attano āyusaṅkhāre upadhāretvā bhikkhusaṅghamāha – "tumhehi kathaṃ parinibbāyantā bhikkhū diṭṭhapubbā"ti.
rows.english: After reciting the Pātimokkha, it seems, on the Uposatha day of the full moon, one of the two elders who were brothers went to his own dwelling place surrounded by the Community of Bhikkhus. As he stood on the walk looking at the moonlight he calculated his own vital formations, and he said to the Community of Bhikkhus, “In what way have you seen bhikkhus attaining Nibbāna up till now?”

rows.pali: Tatra keci āhaṃsu "amhehi āsane nisinnakāva parinibbāyantā diṭṭhapubbā"ti.
rows.english: Some answered, “Till now we have seen them attain Nibbāna sitting in their seats.”

rows.pali: Keci "amhehi ākāse pallaṅkamābhujitvā nisinnakā"ti.
rows.english: Others answered, “We have seen them sitting cross-legged in the air.”

rows.pali: Thero āha – "ahaṃ dāni vo caṅkamantameva parinibbāyamānaṃ dassessāmī"ti tato caṅkame lekhaṃ katvā "ahaṃ ito caṅkamakoṭito parakoṭiṃ gantvā nivattamāno imaṃ lekhaṃ patvāva parinibbāyissāmī"ti vatvā caṅkamaṃ oruyha parabhāgaṃ gantvā nivattamāno ekena pādena lekhaṃ akkantakkhaṇeyeva parinibbāyi.
rows.english: The elder said, “I shall now show you one attaining Nibbāna while walking. ” He then drew a line on the walk, saying, “I shall go from this end of the walk to the other end and return; when I reach this line I shall attain Nibbāna. ” So saying, he stepped on to the walk and went to the far end. On his return he attained Nibbāna in the same moment in which he stepped on the line. [293]

rows.pali: Idaṃ ānāpānassatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with mindfulness of breathing in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 239.Ānāpānassatiyā anantaraṃ uddiṭṭhaṃ pana upasamānussatiṃ bhāvetukāmena rahogatena paṭisallīnena – "yāvatā, bhikkhave, dhammā saṅkhatā vā asaṅkhatā vā virāgo tesaṃ dhammānaṃ aggamakkhāyati, yadidaṃ madanimmadano pipāsavinayo ālayasamugghāto vaṭṭupacchedo taṇhakkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbāna"nti (a. ni. 4.34; itivu. 90) evaṃ sabbadukkhūpasamasaṅkhātassa nibbānassa guṇā anussaritabbā.
rows.english: 245. One who wants to develop the recollection of peace mentioned next to mindfulness of breathing (III.105) should go into solitary retreat and recollect the special qualities of Nibbāna, in other words, the stilling of all suffering, as follows: “Bhikkhus, in so far as there are dhammas, whether formed or unformed, fading away is pronounced the best of them, that is to say, the disillusionment of vanity, the elimination of thirst, the abolition of reliance, the termination of the round, the destruction of craving, fading away, cessation, Nibbāna” (A II 34).

rows.pali: Tattha yāvatāti yattakā.
rows.english: 246. Herein in so far as means as many as.

rows.pali: Dhammāti sabhāvā.
rows.english: Dhammas [means] individual essences.69

rows.pali: Saṅkhatā vā asaṅkhatā vāti saṅgamma samāgamma paccayehi katā vā akatā vā.
rows.english: Whether formed or unformed: whether made by conditions going together, coming together, or not so made.70

rows.pali: Virāgo tesaṃ dhammānaṃaggamakkhāyatīti tesaṃ saṅkhatāsaṅkhatadhammānaṃ virāgo aggamakkhāyati seṭṭho uttamoti vuccati.
rows.english: Fading away is pronounced the best of them: of these formed and unformed dhammas, fading away is pronounced the best, is called the foremost, the highest.

rows.pali: Tattha virāgoti na rāgābhāvamattameva, atha kho yadidaṃ madanimmadano - pe - nibbānanti yo so madanimmadanotiādīni nāmāni asaṅkhatadhammo labhati, so virāgoti paccetabbo.
rows.english: 247. Herein fading away is not mere absence of greed, but rather it is that unformed dhamma which, while given the names “disillusionment of vanity,” etc., in the clause, “that is to say, the disillusionment of vanity, … Nibbāna,” is treated basically as fading away.71

rows.pali: So hi yasmā tamāgamma sabbepi mānamadapurisamadādayo madā nimmadā amadā honti vinassanti, tasmā madanimmadanoti vuccati.
rows.english: It is called disillusionment of vanity because on coming to it all kinds of vanity (intoxication), such as the vanity of conceit, and vanity of manhood, are disillusioned, undone, done away with.72

rows.pali: Yasmā ca tamāgamma sabbāpi kāmapipāsā vinayaṃ abbhatthaṃ yāti, tasmā pipāsavinayoti vuccati.
rows.english: And it is called elimination of thirst because on coming to it all thirst for sense desires is eliminated and quenched.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana tamāgamma pañcakāmaguṇālayā samugghātaṃ gacchanti, tasmā ālayasamugghātoti vuccati.
rows.english: But it is called abolition of reliance because on coming to its reliance on the five cords of sense desire is abolished.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca tamāgamma tebhūmakaṃ vaṭṭaṃ upacchijjati, tasmā vaṭṭupacchedoti vuccati.
rows.english: It is called termination of the round because on coming to it the round of the three planes [of existence] is terminated.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana tamāgamma sabbaso taṇhā khayaṃ gacchati virajjati nirujjhati ca, tasmā taṇhakkhayo virāgo nirodhoti vuccati.
rows.english: It is called destruction of craving because on coming to it craving is entirely destroyed, fades away and ceases.

rows.pali: Yasmā panesa catasso yoniyo pañca gatiyo satta viññāṇaṭṭhitiyo nava ca sattāvāse aparāparabhāvāya vinanato ābandhanato saṃsibbanato vānanti laddhavohārāya taṇhāya nikkhanto nissaṭo visaṃyutto, tasmā nibbānanti vuccatīti.
rows.english: It is called Nibbāna (extinction) because it has gone away from (nikkhanta), has escaped from (nissaṭa), is dissociated from craving, which has acquired in common usage the name “fastening” (vāna) because, by ensuring successive becoming, craving serves as a joining together, a binding together, a lacing together, of the four kinds of generation, five destinies, seven stations of consciousness and nine abodes of beings.73

rows.pali: Evametesaṃ madanimmadanatādīnaṃ guṇānaṃ vasena nibbānasaṅkhāto upasamo anussaritabbo.
rows.english: 248. This is how peace, in other words, Nibbāna, should be recollected according to its special qualities beginning with disillusionment of vanity.

rows.pali: Ye vā panaññepi bhagavatā – "asaṅkhatañca vo, bhikkhave, desessāmi… saccañca… pārañca… sududdasañca… ajarañca… dhuvañca… nippapañcañca… amatañca… sivañca… khemañca… abbhutañca… anītikañca… abyābajjhañca… visuddhiñca… dīpañca… tāṇañca … leṇañca vo, bhikkhave, desessāmī"tiādīsu (saṃ. ni. 4.366) suttesu upasamaguṇā vuttā, tesampi vasena anussaritabboyeva.
rows.english: But it should also be recollected according to the other special qualities of peace stated by the Blessed One in the suttas beginning with: “Bhikkhus, I shall teach you the unformed … the truth … the other shore … the hard-to-see … the undecaying … the lasting … the undiversified … the deathless … the auspicious … the safe … the marvellous … the intact … the unafflicted … the purity … the island … the shelter ….” (S IV 360–72).74

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ madanimmadanatādiguṇavasena upasamaṃ anussarato neva tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti, na dosa… na mohapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 249. As he recollects peace in its special qualities of disillusionment of vanity, etc., in this way, then: “On that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed or obsessed by hate or obsessed by delusion; his mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by peace” (see VII.65, etc.).

rows.pali: Ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti upasamaṃ ārabbhāti buddhānussatiādīsu vuttanayeneva vikkhambhitanīvaraṇassa ekakkhaṇe jhānaṅgāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: So when he has suppressed the hindrances in the way already described under the recollection of the Enlightened One, etc., the jhāna factors arise in a single moment.

rows.pali: Upasamaguṇānaṃ pana gambhīratāya nānappakāraguṇānussaraṇādhimuttatāya vā appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But owing to the profundity of the special qualities of peace, or owing to his being occupied in recollecting special qualities of various kinds, the jhāna is only access and does not reach absorption.

rows.pali: Tadetamupasamaguṇānussaraṇavasena upasamānussaticceva saṅkhyaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that jhāna itself is known as “recollection of peace” too because it arises by means of the special qualities of peace.

rows.pali: Cha anussatiyo viya ca ayampi ariyasāvakasseva ijjhati, evaṃ santepi upasamagarukena puthujjanenāpi manasi kātabbā.
rows.english: 250. And as in the case of the six recollections, this also comes to success only in a noble disciple. Still, though this is so, it can nevertheless also be brought to mind by an ordinary person who values peace.

rows.pali: Sutavasenāpi hi upasame cittaṃ pasīdati.
rows.english: For even by hearsay the mind has confidence in peace.

rows.pali: Imañca pana upasamānussatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu sukhaṃ supati, sukhaṃ paṭibujjhati, santindriyo hoti santamānaso hirottappasamannāgato pāsādiko paṇītādhimuttiko sabrahmacārīnaṃ garu ca bhāvanīyo ca.
rows.english: 251. A bhikkhu who is devoted to this recollection of peace sleeps in bliss and wakes in bliss, his faculties are peaceful, his mind is peaceful, he has conscience and shame, he is confident, he is resolved [to attain] the superior [state], he is respected and honoured by his fellows in the life of purity.

rows.pali: Uttari appaṭivijjhanto pana sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: And even if he penetrates no higher, he is at least headed for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Idaṃ upasamānussatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of peace in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 167.Idāni ito anantarāya maraṇassatiyā bhāvanāniddeso anuppatto.
rows.english: 1.[229] Now comes the description of the development of mindfulness of death, which was listed next (III.105).

rows.pali: Tattha maraṇanti ekabhavapariyāpannassa jīvitindriyassa upacchedo.
rows.english: Herein, death (maraṇa) is the interruption of the life faculty included within [the limits of] a single becoming (existence).

rows.pali: Yaṃ panetaṃ arahantānaṃ vaṭṭadukkhasamucchedasaṅkhātaṃ samucchedamaraṇaṃ, saṅkhārānaṃ khaṇabhaṅgasaṅkhātaṃ khaṇikamaraṇaṃ, rukkho mato lohaṃ matantiādīsu sammutimaraṇañca, na taṃ idha adhippetaṃ.
rows.english: But death as termination (cutting off), in other words, the Arahant’s termination of the suffering of the round, is not intended here, nor is momentary death, in other words, the momentary dissolution of formations, nor the “death” of conventional (metaphorical) usage in such expressions as “dead tree,” “dead metal,” and so on.

rows.pali: Yampi cetaṃ adhippetaṃ, taṃ kālamaraṇaṃ akālamaraṇanti duvidhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 2.As intended here it is of two kinds, that is to say, timely death and untimely death.

rows.pali: Tattha kālamaraṇaṃ puññakkhayena vā āyukkhayena vā ubhayakkhayena vā hoti.
rows.english: Herein, timely death comes about with the exhaustion of merit or with the exhaustion of a life span or with both.

rows.pali: Akālamaraṇaṃ kammupacchedakakammavasena.
rows.english: Untimely death comes about through kamma that interrupts [other, life-producing] kamma.

rows.pali: Tattha yaṃ vijjamānāyapi āyusantānajanakapaccayasampattiyā kevalaṃ paṭisandhijanakassa kammassa vipakkavipākattā maraṇaṃ hoti, idaṃ puññakkhayena maraṇaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 3.Herein, death through exhaustion of merit is a term for the kind of death that comes about owing to the result of [former] rebirth-producing kamma’s having finished ripening although favourable conditions for prolonging the continuity of a life span may be still present.

rows.pali: Yaṃ gatikālāhārādisampattiyā abhāvena ajjatanakālapurisānaṃ viya vassasatamattaparimāṇassa āyuno khayavasena maraṇaṃ hoti, idaṃ āyukkhayena maraṇaṃ nāma.
rows.english: Death through exhaustion of a life span is a term for the kind of death that comes about owing to the exhaustion of the normal life span of men of today, which measures only a century owing to want of such excellence in destiny [as deities have] or in time [as there is at the beginning of an aeon] or in nutriment [as the Uttarakurus and so on have].1

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana dūsīmārakalāburājādīnaṃ viya taṅkhaṇaññeva ṭhānācāvanasamatthena kammunā upacchinnasantānānaṃ, purimakammavasena vā satthaharaṇādīhi upakkamehi upacchijjamānasantānānaṃ maraṇaṃ hoti, idaṃ akālamaraṇaṃ nāma.
rows.english: Untimely death is a term for the death of those whose continuity is interrupted by kamma capable of causing them to fall (cāvana) from their place at that very moment, as in the case of Dūsi-Māra (see M I 337), Kalāburājā (see J-a III 39), etc.,2 or for the death of those whose [life’s] continuity is interrupted by assaults with weapons, etc., due to previous kamma.[230]

rows.pali: Taṃ sabbampi vuttappakārena jīvitindriyupacchedena saṅgahitaṃ.
rows.english: All these are included under the interruption of the life faculty of the kinds already stated.

rows.pali: Iti jīvitindriyupacchedasaṅkhātassa maraṇassa saraṇaṃ maraṇassati.
rows.english: So mindfulness of death is the remembering of death, in other words, of the interruption of the life faculty.

rows.pali: 168.Taṃ bhāvetukāmena rahogatena paṭisallīnena "maraṇaṃ bhavissati, jīvitindriyaṃ upacchijjissatī"ti vā, "maraṇaṃ maraṇa"nti vā yoniso manasikāro pavattetabbo.
rows.english: 4.One who wants to develop this should go into solitary retreat and exercise attention wisely in this way: “Death will take place; the life faculty will be interrupted,” or “Death, death.”

rows.pali: Ayoniso pavattayato hi iṭṭhajanamaraṇānussaraṇe soko uppajjati vijātamātuyā piyaputtamaraṇānussaraṇe viya.
rows.english: 5.If he exercises his attention unwisely in recollecting the [possible] death of an agreeable person, sorrow arises, as in a mother on recollecting the death of her beloved child she bore;

rows.pali: Aniṭṭhajanamaraṇānussaraṇe pāmojjaṃ uppajjati verīnaṃ verimaraṇānussaraṇe viya.
rows.english: and gladness arises in recollecting the death of a disagreeable person, as in enemies on recollecting the death of their enemies;

rows.pali: Majjhattajanamaraṇānussaraṇe saṃvego na uppajjati matakaḷevaradassane chavaḍāhakassa viya.
rows.english: and no sense of urgency arises on recollecting the death of neutral people, as happens in a corpse-burner on seeing a dead body;

rows.pali: Attano maraṇānussaraṇe santāso uppajjati ukkhittāsikaṃ vadhakaṃ disvā bhīrukajātikassa viya.
rows.english: and anxiety arises on recollecting one’s own death, as happens in a timid person on seeing a murderer with a poised dagger.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ sabbampi satisaṃvegañāṇavirahato hoti.
rows.english: 6.In all that there is neither mindfulness nor sense of urgency nor knowledge.

rows.pali: Tasmā tattha tattha hatamatasatte oloketvā diṭṭhapubbasampattīnaṃ sattānaṃ matānaṃ maraṇaṃ āvajjetvā satiñca saṃvegañca ñāṇañca yojetvā "maraṇaṃ bhavissatī"tiādinā nayena manasikāro pavattetabbo.
rows.english: So he should look here and there at beings that have been killed or have died, and advert to the death of beings already dead but formerly seen enjoying good things, doing so with mindfulness, with a sense of urgency and with knowledge, after which he can exercise his attention in the way beginning, “Death will take place.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ pavattento hi yoniso pavatteti, upāyena pavattetīti attho.
rows.english: By so doing he exercises it wisely. He exercises it as a [right] means, is the meaning. 3

rows.pali: Evaṃ pavattayatoyeva hi ekaccassa nīvaraṇāni vikkhambhanti, maraṇārammaṇā sati saṇṭhāti, upacārappattameva kammaṭṭhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 7.When some exercise it merely in this way, their hindrances get suppressed, their mindfulness becomes established with death as its object, and the meditation subject reaches access.

rows.pali: 169.Yassa pana ettāvatā na hoti, tena vadhakapaccupaṭṭhānato, sampattivipattito, upasaṃharaṇato, kāyabahusādhāraṇato, āyudubbalato, animittato, addhānaparicchedato, khaṇaparittatoti imehi aṭṭhahākārehi maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: 8.But one who finds that it does not get so far should do his recollecting of death in eight ways, that is to say: (1) as having the appearance of a murderer, (2) as the ruin of success, (3) by comparison, (4) as to sharing the body with many, (5) as to the frailty of life, (6) as signless, (7) as to the limitedness of the extent, (8) as to the shortness of the moment.

rows.pali: Tattha vadhakapaccupaṭṭhānatoti vadhakassa viya paccupaṭṭhānato.
rows.english: 9.1. Herein, as having the appearance of a murderer: he should do his recollecting thus,

rows.pali: Yathā hi imassa sīsaṃ chindissāmīti asiṃ gahetvā gīvāya cārayamāno vadhako paccupaṭṭhitova hoti, evaṃ maraṇampi paccupaṭṭhitamevāti anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: “Just as a murderer appears with a sword, thinking, ‘I shall cut this man’s head off,’ and applies it to his neck, so death appears.”

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Saha jātiyā āgatato, jīvitaharaṇato ca.
rows.english: Because it comes with birth and it takes away life.

rows.pali: Yathā hi ahicchattakamakuḷaṃ matthakena paṃsuṃ gahetvāva uggacchati, evaṃ sattā jarāmaraṇaṃ gahetvāva nibbattanti.
rows.english: 10.As budding toadstools always come up lifting dust on their tops, so beings are born along with aging and death.

rows.pali: Tathā hi nesaṃ paṭisandhicittaṃ uppādānantarameva jaraṃ patvā pabbatasikharato patitasilā viya bhijjati saddhiṃ sampayuttakhandhehi.
rows.english: For accordingly their rebirth-linking consciousness reaches aging immediately next to its arising and then breaks up together with its associated aggregates, like a stone that falls from the summit of a rock.[231]

rows.pali: Evaṃ khaṇikamaraṇaṃ tāva saha jātiyā āgataṃ.
rows.english: So to begin with, momentary death comes along with birth.

rows.pali: Jātassa pana avassaṃ maraṇato idhādhippetamaraṇampi saha jātiyā āgataṃ.
rows.english: But death is inevitable for what is born; consequently the kind of death intended here also comes along with birth.

rows.pali: Tasmā esa satto jātakālato paṭṭhāya yathā nāma uṭṭhito sūriyo atthābhimukho gacchateva, gatagataṭṭhānato īsakampi na nivattati.
rows.english: 11. Therefore, just as the risen sun moves on towards its setting and never turns back even for a little while from wherever it has got to,

rows.pali: Yathā vā nadī pabbateyyā sīghasotā hārahārinī sandateva vattateva īsakampi na nivattati, evaṃ īsakampi anivattamāno maraṇābhimukhova yāti.
rows.english: or just as a mountain torrent sweeps by with a rapid current, ever flowing and rushing on and never turning back even for a little while, so too this living being travels on towards death from the time when he is born, and he never turns back even for a little while.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ –
rows.english: Hence it is said:

rows.pali: Evaṃ gacchato cassa gimhābhitattānaṃ kunnadīnaṃ khayo viya, pāto āporasānugatabandhanānaṃ dumapphalānaṃ patanaṃ viya, muggarābhitāḷitānaṃ mattikabhājanānaṃ bhedo viya, sūriyarasmisamphuṭṭhānaṃ ussāvabindūnaṃ viddhaṃsanaṃ viya ca maraṇameva āsannaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 12. And whilst he goes on thus death is as near to him as drying up is to rivulets in the summer heat, as falling is to the fruits of trees when the sap reaches their attachments in the morning, as breaking is to clay pots tapped by a mallet, as vanishing is to dewdrops touched by the sun’s rays.

rows.pali: Tenāha –
rows.english: Hence it is said:

rows.pali: Evaṃ ukkhittāsiko vadhako viya saha jātiyā āgataṃ panetaṃ maraṇaṃ gīvāya asiṃ cārayamāno so vadhako viya jīvitaṃ haratiyeva, na aharitvā nivattati.
rows.english: 13.So this death, which comes along with birth, is like a murderer with poised sword. And like the murderer who applies the sword to the neck, it carries off life and never returns to bring it back.

rows.pali: Tasmā saha jātiyā āgatato, jīvitaharaṇato ca ukkhittāsiko vadhako viya maraṇampi paccupaṭṭhitamevāti evaṃ vadhakapaccupaṭṭhānato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: That is why, since death appears like a murderer with poised sword owing to its coming along with birth and carrying off life, it should be recollected as “having the appearance of a murderer. ”

rows.pali: 170.Sampattivipattitoti idha sampatti nāma tāvadeva sobhati, yāva naṃ vipatti nābhibhavati, na ca sā sampatti nāma atthi, yā vipattiṃ atikkamma tiṭṭheyya.
rows.english: 14. 2. As the ruin of success: here success shines as long as failure does not overcome it. And the success does not exist that might endure out of reach of failure.

rows.pali: Tathā hi –
rows.english: Accordingly:

rows.pali: Apica sabbaṃ ārogyaṃ byādhipariyosānaṃ, sabbaṃ yobbanaṃ jarāpariyosānaṃ, sabbaṃ jīvitaṃ maraṇapariyosānaṃ, sabboyeva lokasannivāso jātiyā anugato, jarāya anusaṭo, byādhinā abhibhūto, maraṇena abbhāhato.
rows.english: 15. Furthermore, all health ends in sickness, all youth ends in aging, all life ends in death; all worldly existence is procured by birth, haunted by aging, surprised by sickness, and struck down by death.

rows.pali: Tenāha –
rows.english: Hence it is said:

rows.pali: Evaṃ jīvitasampattiyā maraṇavipattipariyosānataṃ vavatthapentena sampattivipattito maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: This is how death should be recollected as the “ruin of success” by defining it as death’s final ruining of life’s success.

rows.pali: 171.Upasaṃharaṇatoti parehi saddhiṃ attano upasaṃharaṇato.
rows.english: 16. 3. By comparison: by comparing oneself to others.

rows.pali: Tattha sattahākārehi upasaṃharaṇato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ, yasamahattato, puññamahattato, thāmamahattato, iddhimahattato, paññāmahattato, paccekabuddhato, sammāsambuddhatoti.
rows.english: Herein, death should be recollected by comparison in seven ways, that is to say: with those of great fame, with those of great merit, with those of great strength, with those of great supernormal power, with those of great understanding, with Paccekabuddhas, with fully enlightened Buddhas.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Idaṃ maraṇaṃ nāma mahāyasānaṃ mahāparivārānaṃ sampannadhanavāhanānaṃ mahāsammatamandhātumahāsudassana daḷhanemi nimippabhutīnampi upari nirāsaṅkameva patitaṃ, kimaṅgaṃ pana mayhaṃ upari na patissati?
rows.english: 17. Although Mahāsammata, Mandhātu, Mahāsudassana, Daḷhanemi, Nimi,6 etc.,7 were greatly famous and had a great following, and though they had amassed enormous wealth, yet death inevitably caught up with them at length, so how shall it not at length overtake me?

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva yasamahattato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way, firstly, by comparison with those of great fame.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ puññamahattato?
rows.english: 18.How by comparison with those of great merit?

rows.pali: Evaṃ puññamahattato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way by comparison with those of great merit.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ thāmamahattato?
rows.english: 19.How by comparison with those of great strength?

rows.pali: Evaṃ thāmamahattato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way by comparison with those of great strength.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ iddhimahattato?
rows.english: 20.How by comparison with those of great supernormal power?

rows.pali: Evaṃ iddhimahattato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way by comparison with those of great supernormal power.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ paññāmahattato?
rows.english: 21.How by comparison with those of great understanding? [234]

rows.pali: Evaṃ paññāmahattato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way by comparison with those of great understanding.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ paccekabuddhato?
rows.english: 22.How by comparison with Paccekabuddhas?

rows.pali: Yepi te attano ñāṇavīriyabalena sabbakilesasattunimmathanaṃ katvā paccekabodhiṃ pattā khaggavisāṇakappā sayambhuno, tepi maraṇato na muttā, kuto panāhaṃ muccissāmīti.
rows.english: Even those who by the strength of their own knowledge and energy crushed all the enemy defilements and reached enlightenment for themselves, who [stood alone] like the horn of the rhinoceros (see Sn p. 35f.), who were self-perfected, were still not free from death. So how should I be free from it?

rows.pali: Evaṃ paccekabuddhato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way by comparison with Paccekabuddhas.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ sammāsambuddhato?
rows.english: 23.How by comparison with fully enlightened Buddhas?

rows.pali: Yopi so bhagavā asītianubyañjanapaṭimaṇḍitadvattiṃsamahāpurisalakkhaṇavicitrarūpakāyo sabbākāraparisuddhasīlakkhandhādiguṇaratanasamiddhadhammakāyo yasamahattapuññamahattathāmamahattaiddhimahattapaññāmahattānaṃ pāraṃ gato asamo asamasamo appaṭipuggalo arahaṃ sammāsambuddho, sopi salilavuṭṭhinipātena mahāaggikkhandho viya maraṇavuṭṭhinipātena ṭhānaso vūpasanto.
rows.english: Even the Blessed One, whose material body was embellished with the eighty lesser details and adorned with the thirty-two marks of a great man (see MN 91; DN 30), whose Dhamma body brought to perfection the treasured qualities of the aggregates of virtue, etc.,8 made pure in every aspect, who overpassed greatness of fame, greatness of merit, greatness of strength, greatness of supernormal power and greatness of understanding, who had no equal, who was the equal of those without equal, without double, accomplished and fully enlightened—even he was suddenly quenched by the downpour of death’s rain, as a great mass of fire is quenched by the downpour of a rain of water.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sammāsambuddhato anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be recollected in this way by comparison with fully enlightened Buddhas.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ yasamahattatādisampannehi parehi saddhiṃ maraṇasāmaññatāya attānaṃ upasaṃharitvā tesaṃ viya sattavisesānaṃ mayhampi maraṇaṃ bhavissatīti anussarato upacārappattaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ hotīti.
rows.english: 24. When he does his recollecting in this way by comparing himself with others possessed of such great fame, etc., in the light of the universality of death, thinking, “Death will come to me even as it did to those distinguished beings,” then his meditation subject reaches access.

rows.pali: Evaṃ upasaṃharaṇato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: This is how death should be recollected by comparison.

rows.pali: 172.Kāyabahusādhāraṇatoti ayaṃ kāyo bahusādhāraṇo.
rows.english: 25. 4. As to the sharing of the body with many: this body is shared by many.

rows.pali: Asītiyā tāva kimikulānaṃ sādhāraṇo, tattha chavinissitā pāṇā chaviṃ khādanti, cammanissitā cammaṃ khādanti, maṃsanissitā maṃsaṃ khādanti, nhārunissitā nhāruṃ khādanti, aṭṭhinissitā aṭṭhiṃ khādanti, miñjanissitā miñjaṃ khādanti.
rows.english: Firstly, it is shared by the eighty families of worms. There too, creatures live in dependence on the outer skin, feeding on the outer skin; creatures live in dependence on the inner skin, feeding on the inner skin; creatures live in dependence on the flesh, feeding on the flesh; creatures live in dependence on the sinews, feeding on the sinews; creatures live in dependence on the bones, feeding on the bones; and creatures live in dependence on the marrow, feeding on the marrow.

rows.pali: Tattheva jāyanti jīyanti mīyanti, uccārapassāvaṃ karonti.
rows.english: And there they are born, grow old and die, evacuate, and make water;

rows.pali: Kāyova nesaṃ sūtigharañceva gilānasālā ca susānañca vaccakuṭi ca passāvadoṇikā ca.
rows.english: and the body is their maternity home, their hospital, their charnel-ground, their privy and their urinal.

rows.pali: Svāyaṃ tesampi kimikulānaṃ pakopena maraṇaṃ nigacchatiyeva.
rows.english: The body can also be brought to death with the upsetting of these worms.

rows.pali: Yathā ca asītiyā kimikulānaṃ, evaṃ ajjhattikānaṃyeva anekasatānaṃ rogānaṃ bāhirānañca ahivicchikādīnaṃ maraṇassa paccayānaṃ sādhāraṇo.
rows.english: And just as it is shared with the eighty families of worms, so too it is shared by the several hundred internal diseases, as well as by such external causes of death as snakes, scorpions, and what not.

rows.pali: Yathā hi catumahāpathe ṭhapite lakkhamhi sabbadisāhi āgatā sarasattitomarapāsāṇādayo nipatanti, evaṃ kāyepi sabbupaddavā nipatanti.
rows.english: 26.And just as when a target is set up at a crossroads and then arrows, spears, pikes, stones, etc., come from all directions and fall upon it, so too all kinds of accidents befall the body,

rows.pali: Svāyaṃ tesampi upaddavānaṃ nipātena maraṇaṃ nigacchatiyeva.
rows.english: and it also comes to death through these accidents befalling it.

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā – "idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu divase nikkhante rattiyā paṭihitāya iti paṭisañcikkhati, bahukā kho me paccayā maraṇassa, ahi vā maṃ ḍaṃseyya, vicchiko vā maṃ ḍaṃseyya, satapadī vā maṃ ḍaṃseyya, tena me assa kālaṅkiriyā, so mamassa antarāyo, upakkhalitvā vā papateyyaṃ, bhattaṃ vā me bhuttaṃ byāpajjeyya, pittaṃ vā me kuppeyya, semhaṃ vā me kuppeyya, satthakā vā me vātā kuppeyyuṃ, tena me assa kālaṅkiriyā, so mamassa antarāyo"ti.
rows.english: Hence the Blessed One said: “Here, bhikkhus, when day is departing and night is drawing on,9 a bhikkhu considers thus: ‘In many ways I can risk death. A snake may bite me, or a scorpion may sting me, or a centipede may sting me. I might die of that, and that would set me back. Or I might stumble and fall, or the food I have eaten might disagree with me, or my bile might get upset, or my phlegm might get upset [and sever my joints as it were] like knives. I might die of that, and that would set me back’” (A III 306).

rows.pali: Evaṃ (a. ni. 6.20) kāyabahusādhāraṇato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: That is how death should be recollected as to sharing the body with many.

rows.pali: 173.Āyudubbalatoti āyu nāmetaṃ abalaṃ dubbalaṃ.
rows.english: 27. 5. As to the frailty of life: this life is impotent and frail.

rows.pali: Tathā hi sattānaṃ jīvitaṃ assāsapassāsūpanibaddhañceva iriyāpathūpanibaddhañca sītuṇhūpanibaddhañca mahābhūtūpanibaddhañca āhārūpanibaddhañca.
rows.english: For the life of beings is bound up with breathing, it is bound up with the postures, it is bound up with cold and heat, it is bound up with the primary elements, and it is bound up with nutriment.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ assāsapassāsānaṃ samavuttitaṃ labhamānameva pavattati.
rows.english: 28. Life occurs only when the in-breaths and out-breaths occur evenly.

rows.pali: Bahi nikkhantanāsikavāte pana anto apavisante, paviṭṭhe vā anikkhamante mato nāma hoti.
rows.english: But when the wind in the nostrils that has gone outside does not go in again, or when that which has gone inside does not come out again, then a man is reckoned to be dead.

rows.pali: Catunnaṃ iriyāpathānampi samavuttitaṃ labhamānameva pavattati.
rows.english: And it occurs only when the four postures are found occurring evenly. [236]

rows.pali: Aññataraññatarassa pana adhimattatāya āyusaṅkhārā upacchijjanti.
rows.english: But with the prevailing of anyone of them the life process is interrupted.

rows.pali: Sītuṇhānampi samavuttitaṃ labhamānameva pavattati.
rows.english: And it occurs only when cold and heat are found occurring evenly.

rows.pali: Atisītena pana atiuṇhena vā abhibhūtassa vipajjati.
rows.english: But it fails when a man is overcome by excessive cold or heat.

rows.pali: Mahābhūtānampi samavuttitaṃ labhamānameva pavattati.
rows.english: And it occurs only when the four primary elements are found occurring evenly.

rows.pali: Pathavīdhātuyā pana āpodhātuādīnaṃ vā aññataraññatarassa pakopena balasampannopi puggalo patthaddhakāyo vā atisārādivasena kilinnapūtikāyo vā mahāḍāhapareto vā sambhijjamānasandhibandhano vā hutvā jīvitakkhayaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: But with the disturbance of the earth element even a strong man’s life can be terminated if his body becomes rigid, or with the disturbance of one of the elements beginning with water if his body becomes flaccid and petrified with a flux of the bowels, etc., or if he is consumed by a bad fever, or if he suffers a severing of his limb-joint ligatures (cf. XI.102).

rows.pali: Kabaḷīkārāhārampi yuttakāle labhantasseva jīvitaṃ pavattati, āhāraṃ alabhamānassa pana parikkhayaṃ gacchatīti.
rows.english: And life occurs only in one who gets physical nutriment at the proper time; but if he gets none, he uses his life up.

rows.pali: Evaṃ āyudubbalato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: This is how death should be recollected as to the frailty of life.

rows.pali: 174.Animittatoti avavatthānato, paricchedābhāvatoti attho.
rows.english: 29. 6. As signless: as indefinable. The meaning is that it is unpredictable.

rows.pali: Sattānaṃ hi –
rows.english: For in the case of all beings:

rows.pali: Tattha jīvitaṃ tāva "ettakameva jīvitabbaṃ, na ito para"nti vavatthānābhāvato animittaṃ.
rows.english: 30.Herein, firstly the span has no sign because there is no definition such as: Just so much must be lived, no more than that.

rows.pali: Kalalakālepi hi sattā maranti, abbudapesighanamāsikadvemāsatemāsacatumāsapañcamāsadasamāsakālepi.
rows.english: For beings [die in the various stages of the embryo, namely], at the time of the kalala, of the abbuda, of the pesi, of the ghana, at one month gone, two months gone, three months gone, four months gone, five months gone … ten months gone,

rows.pali: Kucchito nikkhantasamayepi.
rows.english: and on the occasion of coming out of the womb.

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ vassasatassa antopi bahipi marantiyeva.
rows.english: And after that they die this side or the other of the century.

rows.pali: Byādhipi "imināva byādhinā sattā maranti, nāññenā"ti vavatthānābhāvato animitto.
rows.english: 31.And the sickness has no sign because there is no definition such as: Beings die only of this sickness, not of any other.

rows.pali: Cakkhurogenāpi hi sattā maranti, sotarogādīnaṃ aññatarenāpi.
rows.english: For beings die of eye disease or of any one among those beginning with ear disease (see A V 110).

rows.pali: Kālopi "imasmiṃyeva kāle maritabbaṃ, nāññasmi"nti evaṃ vavatthānābhāvato animitto.
rows.english: 32.And the time has no sign because there is no definition such as: One has to die only at this time, not at any other.

rows.pali: Pubbaṇhepi hi sattā maranti, majjhanhikādīnaṃ aññatarasmimpi.
rows.english: For beings die in the morning and at any of the other times such as noon.

rows.pali: Dehanikkhepanampi "idheva mīyamānānaṃ dehena patitabbaṃ, nāññatrā"ti evaṃ vavatthānābhāvato animittaṃ.
rows.english: 33. And where the body will be laid down has no sign because there is no definition such as: When people die, they must drop their bodies only here, not anywhere else.

rows.pali: Antogāme jātānaṃ hi bahigāmepi attabhāvo patati.
rows.english: For the person of those born inside a village is dropped outside the village,

rows.pali: Bahigāme jātānampi antogāme.
rows.english: and that of those born outside the village is dropped inside it.

rows.pali: Tathā thalajānaṃ vā jale, jalajānaṃ vā thaleti anekappakārato vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: Likewise that of those born in water is dropped on land, and that of those born on land in water. And this can be multiplied in many ways.

rows.pali: Gatipi "ito cutena idha nibbattitabba"nti evaṃ vavatthānābhāvato animittā.
rows.english: 34. And the destiny has no sign because there is no definition such as: One who dies there must be reborn here.

rows.pali: Devalokato hi cutā manussesupi nibbattanti, manussalokato cutā devalokādīnampi yattha katthaci nibbattantīti evaṃ yantayuttagoṇo viya gatipañcake loko samparivattatīti evaṃ animittato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: For there are some who die in a divine world and are reborn in the human world, and there are some who die in the human world and are reborn in a divine world, and so on. And in this way the world goes round and round the five kinds of destinies like an ox harnessed to a machine. This is how death should be recollected as signless.

rows.pali: 175.Addhānaparicchedatoti manussānaṃ jīvitassa nāma etarahi paritto addhā.
rows.english: 35. 7. As to the limitedness of the extent: the extent of human life is short now.

rows.pali: Yo ciraṃ jīvati, so vassasataṃ, appaṃ vā bhiyyo.
rows.english: One who lives long lives a hundred years, more or less.

rows.pali: Tenāha bhagavā – "appamidaṃ, bhikkhave, manussānaṃ āyu, gamanīyo samparāyo, kattabbaṃ kusalaṃ, caritabbaṃ brahmacariyaṃ, natthi jātassa amaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: Hence the Blessed One said: “Bhikkhus, this human life span is short. There is a new life to be gone to, there are profitable [deeds] to be done, there is the life of purity to be led. There is no not dying for the born.

rows.pali: Yo, bhikkhave, ciraṃ jīvati, so vassasataṃ, appaṃ vā bhiyyoti.
rows.english: He who lives long lives a hundred years, more or less …”

rows.pali: Aparampi āha – "bhūtapubbaṃ, bhikkhave, arako nāma satthā ahosī"ti sabbampi sattahi upamāhi alaṅkataṃ suttaṃ vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: And he said further: “Bhikkhus, there was once a teacher called Araka …” (A IV 136), all of which sutta should be given in full, adorned as it is with seven similes.

rows.pali: Aparampi āha – "yocāyaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāveti, aho vatāhaṃ rattindivaṃ jīveyyaṃ, bhagavato sāsanaṃ manasikareyyaṃ, bahuṃ vata me kataṃ assāti.
rows.english: 36. And he said further: “Bhikkhus, when a bhikkhu develops mindfulness of death thus, ‘Oh, let me live a night and day that I may attend to the Blessed One’s teaching, surely much could be done by me,’

rows.pali: Yocāyaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāveti, aho vatāhaṃ divasaṃ jīveyyaṃ, bhagavato sāsanaṃ manasikareyyaṃ, bahuṃ vata me kataṃ assāti.
rows.english: and when a bhikkhu develops mindfulness of death thus, ‘Oh, let me live a day that I may attend to the Blessed One’s teaching, surely much could be done by me,’

rows.pali: Yo cāyaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāveti, aho vatāhaṃ tadantaraṃ jīveyyaṃ, yadantaraṃ ekaṃ piṇḍapātaṃ bhuñjāmi, bhagavato sāsanaṃ manasikareyyaṃ, bahuṃ vata me kataṃ assāti.

Yo cāyaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāveti, aho vatāhaṃ tadantaraṃ jīveyyaṃ, yadantaraṃ cattāro pañca ālope saṅkhāditvā ajjhoharāmi, bhagavato sāsanaṃ manasikareyyaṃ, bahuṃ vata me kataṃ assāti.
rows.english: and when a bhikkhu develops mindfulness of death thus, ‘Oh, let me live as long as it takes to chew and swallow four or five mouthfuls that I may attend to the Blessed One’s teaching, surely much could be done by me’

rows.pali: Ime vuccanti, bhikkhave, bhikkhū pamattā viharanti, dandhaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāventi āsavānaṃ khayāya.
rows.english: —these are called bhikkhus who dwell in negligence and slackly develop mindfulness of death for the destruction of cankers. [238]

rows.pali: Yo ca khvāyaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāveti, aho vatāhaṃ tadantaraṃ jīveyyaṃ, yadantaraṃ ekaṃ ālopaṃ saṅkhāditvā ajjhoharāmi, bhagavato sāsanaṃ manasikareyyaṃ, bahuṃ vata me kataṃ assāti.
rows.english: 37. “And, bhikkhus, when a bhikkhu develops mindfulness of death thus, ‘Oh, let me live for as long as it takes to chew and swallow a single mouthful that I may attend to the Blessed One’s teaching, surely much could be done by me,’

rows.pali: Yo cāyaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāveti, aho vatāhaṃ tadantaraṃ jīveyyaṃ, yadantaraṃ assasitvā vā passasāmi, passasitvā vā assasāmi, bhagavato sāsanaṃ manasikareyyaṃ, bahuṃ vata me kataṃ assāti.
rows.english: and when a bhikkhu develops mindfulness of death thus, ‘Oh, let me live as long as it takes to breathe in and breathe out, or as long as it takes to breathe out and breathe in, that I may attend to the Blessed One’s teaching, surely much could be done by me’

rows.pali: Ime vuccanti, bhikkhave, bhikkhū appamattā viharanti, tikkhaṃ maraṇassatiṃ bhāventi āsavānaṃ khayāyā"ti (a. ni. 6.19).
rows.english: —these are called bhikkhus who dwell in diligence and keenly develop mindfulness of death for the destruction of cankers” (A III 305–6).

rows.pali: Evaṃ catupañcālopasaṅkhādanamattaṃ avissāsiyo paritto jīvitassa addhāti evaṃ addhānaparicchedato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: 38.So short in fact is the extent of life that it is not certain even for as long as it takes to chew and swallow four or five mouthfuls.

rows.pali: 176.Khaṇaparittatoti paramatthato hi atiparitto sattānaṃ jīvitakkhaṇo ekacittappavattimattoyeva.
rows.english: 8. As to the shortness of the moment: in the ultimate sense the life-moment of living beings is extremely short, being only as much as the occurrence of a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Yathā nāma rathacakkaṃ pavattamānampi ekeneva nemippadesena pavattati, tiṭṭhamānampi ekeneva tiṭṭhati, evameva ekacittakkhaṇikaṃ sattānaṃ jīvitaṃ.
rows.english: Just as a chariot wheel, when it is rolling, rolls [that is, touches the ground] only on one point of [the circumference of] its tire, and, when it is at rest, rests only on one point, so too, the life of living beings lasts only for a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ citte niruddhamatte satto niruddhoti vuccati.
rows.english: When that consciousness has ceased, the being is said to have ceased,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "atīte cittakkhaṇe jīvittha, na jīvati, na jīvissati.
rows.english: according as it is said: “In a past conscious moment he did live, not he does live, not he will live.

rows.pali: Anāgate cittakkhaṇe na jīvittha, na jīvati, jīvissati.
rows.english: In a future conscious moment not he did live, not he does live, he will live.

rows.pali: Paccuppanne cittakkhaṇe na jīvittha, jīvati, na jīvissati.
rows.english: In the present conscious moment not he did live, he does live, not he will live.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ khaṇaparittato maraṇaṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: This is how death should be recollected as to the shortness of the moment.

rows.pali: 177.Iti imesaṃ aṭṭhannaṃ ākārānaṃ aññataraññatarena anussaratopi punappunaṃ manasikāravasena cittaṃ āsevanaṃ labhati, maraṇārammaṇā sati santiṭṭhati, nīvaraṇāni vikkhambhanti, jhānaṅgāni pātubhavanti.
rows.english: 40.So while he does his recollecting by means of one or other of these eight ways, his consciousness acquires [the support of] repetition owing to the reiterated attention, mindfulness settles down with death as its object, the hindrances are suppressed, and the jhāna factors make their appearance.

rows.pali: Sabhāvadhammattā pana saṃvejanīyattā ca ārammaṇassa appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But since the object is stated with individual essences,12 and since it awakens a sense of urgency, the jhāna does not reach absorption and is only access. [239]

rows.pali: Lokuttarajjhānaṃ pana dutiyacatutthāni ca āruppajjhānāni sabhāvadhammepi bhāvanāvisesena appanaṃ pāpuṇanti.
rows.english: Now, with special development, the supramundane jhāna and the second and the fourth immaterial jhānas reach absorption even with respect to states with individual essences.

rows.pali: Visuddhibhāvanānukkamavasena hi lokuttaraṃ appanaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: For the supramundane reaches absorption by means of progressive development of the purification

rows.pali: Ārammaṇātikkamabhāvanāvasena āruppaṃ.
rows.english: and the immaterial jhānas do so by means of development consisting in the surmounting of the object (see Ch. X)

rows.pali: Appanāpattasseva hi jhānassa ārammaṇasamatikkamanamattaṃ tattha hoti.
rows.english: since there [in those two immaterial jhānas] there is merely the surmounting of the object of jhāna that had already reached absorption.

rows.pali: Idha pana tadubhayampi natthi.
rows.english: But here [in mundane mindfulness of death] there is neither

rows.pali: Tasmā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: so the jhāna only reaches access.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ maraṇassatibalena uppannattā maraṇassaticceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that access is known as “mindfulness of death” too since it arises through its means.

rows.pali: Imañca pana maraṇassatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu satataṃ appamatto hoti, sabbabhavesu anabhiratisaññaṃ paṭilabhati, jīvitanikantiṃ jahāti, pāpagarahī hoti, asannidhibahulo parikkhāresu vigatamalamacchero, aniccasaññā cassa paricayaṃ gacchati, tadanusāreneva dukkhasaññā anattasaññā ca upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: 41. A bhikkhu devoted to mindfulness of death is constantly diligent. He acquires perception of disenchantment with all kinds of becoming (existence). He conquers attachment to life. He condemns evil. He avoids much storing. He has no stain of avarice about requisites. Perception of impermanence grows in him, following upon which there appear the perceptions of pain and not-self.

rows.pali: Yathā abhāvitamaraṇā sattā sahasā vāḷamigayakkhasappacoravadhakābhibhūtā viya maraṇasamaye bhayaṃ santāsaṃ sammohaṃ āpajjanti, evaṃ anāpajjitvā abhayo asammūḷho kālaṃ karoti.
rows.english: But while beings who have not developed [mindfulness of] death fall victims to fear, horror and confusion at the time of death as though suddenly seized by wild beasts, spirits, snakes, robbers, or murderers, he dies undeluded and fearless without falling into any such state.

rows.pali: Sace diṭṭheva dhamme amataṃ nārādheti, kāyassa bhedā sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: And if he does not attain the deathless here and now, he is at least headed for a happy destiny on the breakup of the body.

rows.pali: Idaṃ maraṇassatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of death in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 178.Idāni yaṃ taṃ aññatra buddhuppādā appavattapubbaṃ sabbatitthiyānaṃ avisayabhūtaṃ tesu tesu suttantesu "ekadhammo, bhikkhave, bhāvito bahulīkato mahato saṃvegāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: 42. Now comes the description of the development of mindfulness occupied with the body as a meditation subject, which is never promulgated except after an Enlightened One’s arising, and is outside the province of any sectarians. It has been commended by the Blessed One in various ways in different suttas thus: “Bhikkhus, when one thing is developed and repeatedly practiced, it leads to a supreme sense of urgency,

rows.pali: Mahato atthāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: to supreme benefit,

rows.pali: Mahato yogakkhemāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: to supreme surcease of bondage,

rows.pali: Mahato satisampajaññāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: to supreme mindfulness and full awareness,

rows.pali: Ñāṇadassanapaṭilābhāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: to acquisition of knowledge and vision,

rows.pali: Diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: to a happy life here and now,

rows.pali: Vijjāvimuttiphalasacchikiriyāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: to realization of the fruit of clear vision and deliverance.

rows.pali: Katamo ekadhammo?
rows.english: What is that one thing?

rows.pali: Kāyagatā sati… (a. ni. 1.563 ādayo).
rows.english: It is mindfulness occupied with the body” (A I 43).

rows.pali: Amataṃ te, bhikkhave, paribhuñjanti, ye kāyagatāsatiṃ paribhuñjanti.
rows.english: And thus: “Bhikkhus, they savour the deathless who savour mindfulness occupied with the body;

rows.pali: Amataṃ te, bhikkhave, na paribhuñjanti, ye kāyagatāsatiṃ na paribhuñjanti.
rows.english: they do not savour the deathless who do not savour mindfulness occupied with the body.13

rows.pali: Amataṃ tesaṃ, bhikkhave, paribhuttaṃ… aparibhuttaṃ… parihīnaṃ… aparihīnaṃ… viraddhaṃ… aviraddhaṃ, yesaṃ kāyagatāsati āraddhāti (a. ni. 1.603) evaṃ bhagavatā anekehi ākārehi pasaṃsitvā "kathaṃ bhāvitā, bhikkhave, kāyagatāsati kathaṃ bahulīkatā mahapphalā hoti mahānisaṃsā?
rows.english: They have savoured the deathless who have savoured mindfulness occupied with the body; they have not savoured … They have neglected … they have not neglected … They have missed … they have found the deathless who have found mindfulness occupied with the body” (A I 45). And it has been described in fourteen sections in the passage beginning, “And how developed, bhikkhus, how repeatedly practiced is mindfulness occupied with the body of great fruit, of great benefit?

rows.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu araññagato vā"tiādinā (ma. ni. 3.154) nayena ānāpānapabbaṃ, iriyāpathapabbaṃ, catusampajaññapabbaṃ, paṭikkūlamanasikārapabbaṃ, dhātumanasikārapabbaṃ, navasivathikapabbānīti imesaṃ cuddasannaṃ pabbānaṃ vasena kāyagatāsatikammaṭṭhānaṃ niddiṭṭhaṃ, tassa bhāvanāniddeso anuppatto.
rows.english: Here, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu, gone to the forest …” (M III 89), that is to say, the sections on breathing, on postures, on the four kinds of full awareness, on attention directed to repulsiveness, on attention directed to elements, and on the nine charnel-ground contemplations.

rows.pali: Tattha yasmā iriyāpathapabbaṃ catusampajaññapabbaṃ dhātumanasikārapabbanti imāni tīṇi vipassanāvasena vuttāni.
rows.english: 43.Herein, the three, that is to say, the sections on postures, on the four kinds of full awareness (see M-a I 253f. ), and on attention directed to elements, as they are stated [in that sutta], deal with insight.

rows.pali: Nava sivathikapabbāni vipassanāñāṇesuyeva ādīnavānupassanāvasena vuttāni.
rows.english: Then the nine sections on the charnel- ground contemplations, as stated there, deal with that particular phase of insight knowledge called contemplation of danger.

rows.pali: Yāpi cettha uddhumātakādīsu samādhibhāvanā ijjheyya, sā asubhaniddese pakāsitāyeva.
rows.english: And any development of concentration in the bloated, etc., that might be implied there has already been explained in the Description of Foulness (Ch. VI).

rows.pali: Ānāpānapabbaṃ pana paṭikkūlamanasikārapabbañca imānevettha dve samādhivasena vuttāni.
rows.english: So there are only the two, that is, the sections on breathing and on directing attention to repulsiveness, that, as stated there, deal with concentration.

rows.pali: Tesu ānāpānapabbaṃ ānāpānassativasena visuṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃyeva.
rows.english: Of these two, the section on breathing is a separate meditation subject, namely, mindfulness of breathing.

rows.pali: Yaṃ panetaṃ "puna caparaṃ, bhikkhave, bhikkhu imameva kāyaṃ uddhaṃ pādatalā adho kesamatthakā tacapariyantaṃ pūraṃ nānappakārassa asucino paccavekkhati.
rows.english: [Text] 44.What is intended here as mindfulness occupied with the body is the thirty- two aspects. This meditation subject is taught as the direction of attention to repulsiveness thus: “Again, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu reviews this body, up from the soles of the feet and down from the top of the hair and contained in the skin, as full of many kinds of filth thus:

rows.pali: Atthi imasmiṃ kāye kesā lomā - pe - mutta"nti (ma. ni. 3.154) evaṃ matthaluṅgaṃ aṭṭhimiñjena saṅgahetvā paṭikkūlamanasikāravasena desitaṃ dvattiṃsākārakammaṭṭhānaṃ, idamidha kāyagatāsatīti adhippetaṃ.
rows.english: In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidney, heart, liver, midriff, spleen, lungs, bowels, entrails, gorge, dung, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, spittle, snot, oil of the joints, and urine” (M III 90), the brain being included in the bone marrow in this version [with a total of only thirty-one aspects].

rows.pali: 179.Tatthāyaṃ pāḷivaṇṇanāpubbaṅgamo bhāvanāniddeso.
rows.english: 45.Here is the description of the development introduced by a commentary on the text.

rows.pali: Imameva kāyanti imaṃ catumahābhūtikaṃ pūtikāyaṃ.
rows.english: [Word Commentary] This body: this filthy body constructed out of the four primary elements.

rows.pali: Uddhaṃ pādatalāti pādatalato upari.
rows.english: Up from the soles of the feet: from the soles of the feet upwards.

rows.pali: Adho kesamatthakāti kesaggato heṭṭhā.
rows.english: Down from the top of the hair: from the highest part of the hair downwards.

rows.pali: Tacapariyantanti tiriyaṃ tacaparicchinnaṃ.
rows.english: Contained in the skin: terminated all round by the skin.

rows.pali: Pūraṃ nānappakārassa asucino paccavekkhatīti nānappakārakesādiasucibharito ayaṃ kāyoti passati.
rows.english: Reviews … as full of many kinds of filth: [241] he sees that this body is packed with the filth of various kinds beginning with head hairs.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Atthi imasmiṃ kāye kesā - pe - muttanti.
rows.english: “In this body there are head hairs … urine. ”

rows.pali: Tattha atthīti saṃvijjanti.
rows.english: 46. Herein, there are means, there are found.

rows.pali: Imasminti yvāyaṃ uddhaṃ pādatalā adho kesamatthakā tacapariyanto pūro nānappakārassa asucinoti vuccati, tasmiṃ.
rows.english: In this: in this, which is expressed thus: “Up from the soles of the feet and down from the top of the hair and contained in the skin, as full of many kinds of filth.

rows.pali: Kāyeti sarīre.
rows.english: ” Body: the carcass;

rows.pali: Sarīraṃ hi asucisañcayato kucchitānaṃ kesādīnañceva cakkhurogādīnañca rogasatānaṃ āyabhūtato kāyoti vuccati.
rows.english: for it is the carcass that is called “body” (kāya) because it is a conglomeration of filth, because such vile (kucchita) things as the head hairs, etc., and the hundred diseases beginning with eye disease, have it as their origin (āya).

rows.pali: Kesā lomāti ete kesādayo dvattiṃsākārā.
rows.english: Head hairs, body hairs: these things beginning with head hairs are the thirty- two aspects.

rows.pali: Tattha atthi imasmiṃ kāye kesā, atthi imasmiṃ kāye lomāti evaṃ sambandho veditabbo.
rows.english: The construction here should be understood in this way: In this body there are head hairs, in this body there are body hairs.

rows.pali: Imasmiṃ hi pādatalā paṭṭhāya upari, kesamatthakā paṭṭhāya heṭṭhā, tacato paṭṭhāya paritoti ettake byāmamatte kaḷevare sabbākārenapi vicinanto na koci kiñci muttaṃ vā maṇiṃ vā veḷuriyaṃ vā agaruṃ vā kuṅkumaṃ vā kappūraṃ vā vāsacuṇṇādiṃ vā aṇumattampi sucibhāvaṃ passati, atha kho paramaduggandhajegucchaṃ asirikadassanaṃ nānappakāraṃ kesalomādibhedaṃ asuciṃyeva passati.
rows.english: 47. No one who searches throughout the whole of this fathom-long carcass, starting upwards from the soles of the feet, starting downwards from the top of the head, and starting from the skin all round, ever finds even the minutest atom at all beautiful in it, such as a pearl, or a gem, or beryl, or aloes,14 or saffron, or camphor, or talcum powder; on the contrary he finds nothing but the various very malodorous, offensive, drab-looking sorts of filth consisting of the head hairs, body hairs, and the rest.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "atthi imasmiṃ kāye kesā lomā - pe - mutta"nti.
rows.english: Hence it is said: “In this body there are head hairs, body hairs … urine. ”

rows.pali: Ayamettha padasambandhato vaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This is the commentary on the word-construction here.

rows.pali: 180.Imaṃ pana kammaṭṭhānaṃ bhāvetukāmena ādikammikena kulaputtena vuttappakāraṃ kalyāṇamittaṃ upasaṅkamitvā idaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: [Development] 48. Now, a clansman who, as a beginner, wants to develop this meditation subject should go to a good friend of the kind already described (III. 61–73) and learn it.

rows.pali: Tenāpissa kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathentena sattadhā uggahakosallaṃ dasadhā ca manasikārakosallaṃ ācikkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And the teacher who expounds it to him should tell him the sevenfold skill in learning and the tenfold skill in giving attention.

rows.pali: Tattha vacasā manasā vaṇṇato saṇṭhānato disato okāsato paricchedatoti evaṃ sattadhā uggahakosallaṃ ācikkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: [The Sevenfold Skill in Learning] Herein, the sevenfold skill in learning should be told thus: (1) as verbal recitation, (2) as mental recitation, (3) as to colour, (4) as to shape, (5) as to direction, (6) as to location, (7) as to delimitation.

rows.pali: Imasmiṃ hi paṭikkūlamanasikārakammaṭṭhāne yopi tipiṭako hoti, tenāpi manasikārakāle paṭhamaṃ vācāya sajjhāyo kātabbo.
rows.english: 49. 1. This meditation subject consists in giving attention to repulsiveness. Even if one is master of the Tipiṭaka, the verbal recitation should still be done at the time of first giving it attention.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa hi sajjhāyaṃ karontasseva kammaṭṭhānaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti malayavāsī mahādevattherassa santike uggahitakammaṭṭhānānaṃ dvinnaṃ therānaṃ viya.
rows.english: For the meditation subject only becomes evident to some through recitation, as it did to the two elders who learned the meditation subject from the Elder Mahā Deva of the Hill Country (Malaya).

rows.pali: Thero kira tehi kammaṭṭhānaṃ yācito cattāro māse imaṃyeva sajjhāyaṃ karothāti dvattiṃsākārapāḷiṃ adāsi.
rows.english: On being asked for the meditation subject, it seems, the elder [242] gave the text of the thirty-two aspects, saying, “Do only this recitation for four months.

rows.pali: Te kiñcāpi nesaṃ dve tayo nikāyā paguṇā, padakkhiṇaggāhitāya pana cattāro māse dvattiṃsākāraṃ sajjhāyantāva sotāpannā ahesuṃ.
rows.english: ” Although they were familiar respectively with two and three Piṭakas, it was only at the end of four months of recitation of the meditation subject that they became stream-enterers, with right apprehension [of the text].

rows.pali: Tasmā kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathentena ācariyena antevāsiko vattabbo "paṭhamaṃ tāva vācāya sajjhāyaṃ karohī"ti.
rows.english: So the teacher who expounds the meditation subject should tell the pupil to do the recitation verbally first.

rows.pali: Karontena ca tacapañcakādīni paricchinditvā anulomapaṭilomavasena sajjhāyo kātabbo.
rows.english: 50.Now, when he does the recitation, he should divide it up into the “skin pentad,” etc., and do it forwards and backwards.

rows.pali: Kesā lomā nakhā dantā tacoti hi vatvā puna paṭilomato taco dantā nakhā lomā kesāti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: After saying “Head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin,” he should repeat it backwards, “Skin, teeth, nails, body hairs, head hairs. ”

rows.pali: Tadanantaraṃ vakkapañcake maṃsaṃ nhāru aṭṭhi aṭṭhimiñjaṃ vakkanti vatvā puna paṭilomato vakkaṃ aṭṭhimiñjaṃ aṭṭhi nhāru maṃsaṃ, taco dantā nakhā lomā kesāti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 51.Next to that, with the “kidney pentad,” after saying “Flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidney,” he should repeat it backwards, “Kidney, bone marrow, bones, sinews, flesh; skin, teeth, nails, body hairs, head hairs. ”

rows.pali: Tato papphāsapañcake hadayaṃ yakanaṃ kilomakaṃ pihakaṃ papphāsanti vatvā puna paṭilomato papphāsaṃ pihakaṃ kilomakaṃ yakanaṃ hadayaṃ, vakkaṃ aṭṭhimiñjaṃ aṭṭhi nhāru maṃsaṃ, taco dantā nakhā lomā kesāti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 52.Next, with the “lungs pentad,” after saying “Heart, liver, midriff, spleen, lungs,” he should repeat it backwards, “Lungs, spleen, midriff, liver, heart; kidney, bone marrow, bones, sinews, flesh; skin, teeth, nails, body hairs, head hairs. ”

rows.pali: Tato matthaluṅgapañcake antaṃ antaguṇaṃ udariyaṃ karīsaṃ matthaluṅganti vatvā puna paṭilomato matthaluṅgaṃ karīsaṃ udariyaṃ antaguṇaṃ antaṃ, papphāsaṃ pihakaṃ kilomakaṃ yakanaṃ hadayaṃ, vakkaṃ aṭṭhimiñjaṃ aṭṭhi nhāru maṃsaṃ, taco dantā nakhā lomā kesāti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 53.Next, with the “brain pentad,” after saying “Bowels, entrails, gorge, dung, brain,” he should repeat it backwards, “Brain, dung, gorge, entrails, bowels; lungs, spleen, midriff, liver, heart; kidney, bone marrow, bones, sinews, flesh; skin, teeth, nails, body hairs, head hairs. ”

rows.pali: Tato medachakke pittaṃ semhaṃ pubbo lohitaṃ sedo medoti vatvā puna paṭilomato medo sedo lohitaṃ pubbo semhaṃ pittaṃ, matthaluṅgaṃ karīsaṃ udariyaṃ antaguṇaṃ antaṃ, papphāsaṃ pihakaṃ kilomakaṃ yakanaṃ hadayaṃ, vakkaṃ aṭṭhimiñjaṃ aṭṭhi nhāru maṃsaṃ, taco dantā nakhā lomā kesāti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 54.Next, with the “fat sextad,” after saying “Bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat,” he should repeat it backwards, “Fat, sweat, blood, pus, phlegm, bile; brain, dung, gorge, entrails, bowels; lungs, spleen, midriff, liver, heart; kidney, bone marrow, bones, sinews, flesh; skin, teeth, nails, body hairs, head hairs. ”

rows.pali: Tato muttachakke assu vasā kheḷo siṅghāṇikā lasikā muttanti vatvā puna paṭilomato muttaṃ lasikā siṅghāṇikā kheḷo vasā assu, medo sedo lohitaṃ pubbo semhaṃ pittaṃ, matthaluṅgaṃ karīsaṃ udariyaṃ antaguṇaṃ antaṃ, papphāsaṃ pihakaṃ kilomakaṃ yakanaṃ hadayaṃ, vakkaṃ aṭṭhimiñjaṃ aṭṭhi nhāru maṃsaṃ, taco dantā nakhā lomā kesāti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 55.Next, with the “urine sextad,” after saying “Tears, grease, spittle, snot, oil of the joints, urine,” he should repeat it backwards, “Urine, oil of the joints, snot, spittle, grease, tears; fat, sweat, blood, pus, phlegm, bile; brain, dung, gorge, entrails, bowels; lungs, spleen, midriff, liver, heart; kidney, bone marrow, bones, sinews, flesh; skin, teeth, nails, body hairs, head hairs. ” [243]

rows.pali: Evaṃ kālasataṃ kālasahassaṃ kālasatasahassampi vācāya sajjhāyo kātabbo.
rows.english: 56. The recitation should be done verbally in this way a hundred times, a thousand times, even a hundred thousand times.

rows.pali: Vacasā sajjhāyena hi kammaṭṭhānatanti paguṇā hoti, na ito cito ca cittaṃ vidhāvati.
rows.english: For it is through verbal recitation that the meditation subject becomes familiar, and the mind being thus prevented from running here and there,

rows.pali: Koṭṭhāsā pākaṭā honti, hatthasaṅkhalikā viya vatipādapanti viya ca khāyanti.
rows.english: the parts become evident and seem like [the fingers of] a pair of clasped hands,15 like a row of fence posts.

rows.pali: Yathā pana vacasā, tatheva manasāpi sajjhāyo kātabbo.
rows.english: 57.2. The mental recitation should be done just as it is done verbally.

rows.pali: Vacasā sajjhāyo hi manasā sajjhāyassa paccayo hoti.
rows.english: For the verbal recitation is a condition for the mental recitation,

rows.pali: Manasā sajjhāyo lakkhaṇapaṭivedhassa paccayo hoti.
rows.english: and the mental recitation is a condition for the penetration of the characteristic [of foulness]. 16

rows.pali: Vaṇṇatoti kesādīnaṃ vaṇṇo vavatthapetabbo.
rows.english: 58.3. As to colour: the colour of the head hairs, etc., should be defined.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānatoti tesaññeva saṇṭhānaṃ vavatthapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 4. As to shape: their shape should be defined too.

rows.pali: Disatoti imasmiṃ hi sarīre nābhito uddhaṃ uparimadisā, adho heṭṭhimadisā, tasmā ayaṃ koṭṭhāso imissā nāma disāyāti disā vavatthapetabbā.
rows.english: 5. As to direction: in this body, upwards from the navel is the upward direction, and downwards from it is the downward direction. So the direction should be defined thus: “This part is in this direction. ”

rows.pali: Okāsatoti ayaṃ koṭṭhāso imasmiṃ nāma okāse patiṭṭhitoti evaṃ tassa tassa okāso vavatthapetabbo.
rows.english: 6. As to location: the location of this or that part should be defined thus: “This part is established in this location. ”

rows.pali: Paricchedatoti sabhāgaparicchedo visabhāgaparicchedoti dve paricchedā.
