rows.english: As to shape, both are the shape of their locations.

rows.pali: Disato sannicitalohitaṃ uparimāya disāya jātaṃ.
rows.english: As to direction, the stored blood belongs to the upper direction;

rows.pali: Itaraṃ dvisu disāsu jātaṃ.
rows.english: the other belongs to both directions.

rows.pali: Okāsato saṃsaraṇalohitaṃ ṭhapetvā kesalomadantanakhānaṃ maṃsavinimuttaṭṭhānañceva thaddhasukkhacammañca dhamanijālānusārena sabbaṃ upādiṇṇasarīraṃ pharitvā ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: As to location, except for the fleshless parts of the head hairs, body hairs, teeth, nails, and the hard dry skin, the mobile blood permeates the whole of the clung-to (kammically-acquired)35 body by following the network of veins.

rows.pali: Sannicitalohitaṃ yakanaṭṭhānassa heṭṭhābhāgaṃ pūretvā ekapatthapūramattaṃ hadayavakkapapphāsānaṃ upari thokaṃ thokaṃ paggharantaṃ vakkahadayayakanapapphāse temayamānaṃ ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: The stored blood fills the lower part of the liver’s site to the extent of a bowlful, and by its splashing little by little over the heart, kidney and lungs, it keeps the kidney, heart, liver and lungs moist.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ hi vakkahadayādīni atemente sattā pipāsitā honti.
rows.english: For it is when it fails to moisten the kidney, heart, etc., that beings become thirsty.

rows.pali: Paricchedato lohitabhāgena paricchinnaṃ, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded by what appertains to blood …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisoyeva.
207.Sedoti lomakūpādīhi paggharaṇakaāpodhātu.
rows.english: [Sweat] 131. This is the water element that trickles from the pores of the body hairs, and so on.

rows.pali: So vaṇṇato vippasannatilatelavaṇṇo.
rows.english: As to colour, it is the colour of clear sesame oil.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato okāsasaṇṭhāno.
rows.english: As to shape, it is the shape of its location.

rows.pali: Disato dvīsu disāsu jāto.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to both directions.

rows.pali: Okāsato sedassokāso nāma nibaddho natthi, yattha so lohitaṃ viya sadā tiṭṭheyya.
rows.english: As to location, there is no fixed location for sweat where it could always be found like blood.

rows.pali: Yadā pana aggisantāpasūriyasantāpautuvikārādīhi sarīraṃ santapati, tadā udakato abbūḷhamattavisamacchinnabhisamuḷālakumudanāḷakalāpo viya sabbakesalomakūpavivarehi paggharati, tasmā tassa saṇṭhānampi kesalomakūpavivarānaññeva vasena veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: But if the body is heated by the heat of a fire, by the sun’s heat, by a change of temperature, etc., then it trickles from all the pore openings of the head hairs and body hairs, as water does from a bunch of unevenly cut lily-bud stems and lotus stalks pulled up from the water. So its shape should also be understood to correspond to the pore-openings of the head hairs and body hairs.

rows.pali: Sedapariggaṇhakena ca yoginā kesalomakūpavivare pūretvā ṭhitavaseneva sedo manasi kātabbo.
rows.english: And the meditator who discerns sweat should only give his attention to it as it is to be found filling the pore-openings of the head hairs and body hairs.

rows.pali: Paricchedato sedabhāgena paricchinno, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded by what appertains to sweat …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisoyeva.
Medoti thinasineho.
rows.english: [Fat] 132. This is a thick unguent.

rows.pali: So vaṇṇato phālitahaliddivaṇṇo.
rows.english: As to colour, it is the colour of sliced turmeric.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato thūlasarīrassa tāva cammamaṃsantare ṭhapitahaliddivaṇṇadukūlapilotikasaṇṭhāno hoti.
rows.english: As to shape, firstly in the body of a stout man it is the shape of turmeric-coloured dukūla (muslin) rags placed between the inner skin and the flesh.

rows.pali: Kisasarīrassa jaṅghamaṃsaṃ ūrumaṃsaṃ piṭṭhikaṇṭakanissitaṃ piṭṭhimaṃsaṃ udaravaṭṭimaṃsanti etāni nissāya diguṇatiguṇaṃ katvā ṭhapitahaliddivaṇṇadukūlapilotikasaṇṭhāno.
rows.english: In the body of a lean man it is the shape of turmeric-coloured dukūla (muslin) rags placed in two or three thicknesses on the shank flesh, thigh flesh, back flesh near the spine, and belly-covering flesh.

rows.pali: Disato dvīsu disāsu jāto.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to both directions.

rows.pali: Okāsato thūlassa sakalasarīraṃ pharitvā kisassa jaṅghamaṃsādīni nissāya ṭhito, yaṃ sinehasaṅkhaṃ gatampi paramajegucchattā neva muddhani telatthāya, na nāsatelādīnamatthāya gaṇhanti.
rows.english: As to location, it permeates the whole of a stout man’s body; it is to be found on a lean man’s shank flesh, and so on. And though it was described as “unguent” above, still it is neither used as oil on the head nor as oil for the nose, etc., because of its utter disgustingness.

rows.pali: Paricchedato heṭṭhā maṃsena, upari cammena, tiriyaṃ medabhāgena paricchinno, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded below by the flesh, above by the inner skin, and all round by what appertains to fat …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisoyeva.
208.Assūti akkhīhi paggharaṇakaāpodhātu.
rows.english: [Tears] 133. These are the water element that trickles from the eye.

rows.pali: Taṃ vaṇṇato vippasannatilatelavaṇṇaṃ.
rows.english: As to colour, they are the colour of clear sesame oil.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato okāsasaṇṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: As to shape, they are the shape of their location.

rows.pali: Disato uparimāya disāya jātaṃ.
rows.english: As to direction, they belong to the upper direction.

rows.pali: Okāsato akkhikūpakesu ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: As to location, they are to be found in the eye sockets.

rows.pali: Na cetaṃ pittakosake pittamiva akkhikūpakesu sadā sannicitaṃ tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: But they are not stored in the eye sockets all the while as the bile is in the bile container.

rows.pali: Yadā pana sattā somanassajātā mahāhasitaṃ hasanti, domanassajātā rodanti paridevanti, tathārūpaṃ vā visamāhāraṃ āhārenti, yadā ca nesaṃ akkhīni dhūmarajapaṃsukādīhi abhihaññanti.
rows.english: But when beings feel joy and laugh uproariously, or feel grief and weep and lament, or eat particular kinds of wrong food, or when their eyes are affected by smoke, dust, dirt, etc.,

rows.pali: Tadā etehi somanassadomanassavisabhāgāhārautūhi samuṭṭhahitvā akkhikūpake pūretvā tiṭṭhati vā paggharati vā.
rows.english: then being originated by the joy, grief, wrong food, or temperature, they fill up the eye sockets or trickle out.

rows.pali: Assupariggaṇhakena ca yoginā akkhikūpake pūretvā ṭhitavaseneva pariggaṇhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And the meditator who discerns tears should discern them only as they are to be found filling the eye sockets.

rows.pali: Paricchedato assubhāgena paricchinnaṃ, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, they are bounded by what appertains to tears …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisoyeva.
209.Vasāti vilīnasineho.
rows.english: [Grease] 134. This is a melted unguent.

rows.pali: Sā vaṇṇato nāḷikeratelavaṇṇā.
rows.english: As to colour, it is the colour of coconut oil.

rows.pali: Ācāme āsittatelavaṇṇātipi vattuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: Also it can be said to be the colour of oil sprinkled on gruel.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato nhānakāle pasannaudakassa upari paribbhamantasinehabinduvisaṭasaṇṭhānā.
rows.english: As to shape, it is a film the shape of a drop of unguent spread out over still water at the time of bathing.

rows.pali: Disato dvīsu disāsu jātā.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to both directions.

rows.pali: Okāsato yebhuyyena hatthatalahatthapiṭṭhipādatalapādapiṭṭhināsapuṭanalāṭaaṃsakūṭesu ṭhitā.
rows.english: As to location, it is to be found mostly on the palms of the hands, backs of the hands, soles of the feet, backs of the feet, tip of the nose, forehead, and points of the shoulders.

rows.pali: Na cesā etesu okāsesu sadā vilīnāva hutvā tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: And it is not always to be found in the melted state in these locations,

rows.pali: Yadā pana aggisantāpasūriyasantāpautuvisabhāgadhātuvisabhāgehi te padesā usmājātā honti, tadā tattha nhānakāle pasannaudakūpari sinehabinduvisaṭo viya ito cito ca sañcarati.
rows.english: but when these parts get hot with the heat of a fire, the sun’s heat, upset of temperature or upset of elements, then it spreads here and there in those places like the film from the drop of unguent on the still water at the time of bathing.

rows.pali: Paricchedato vasābhāgena paricchinnā, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded by what appertains to grease …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisoyeva.
210.Kheḷoti antomukhe pheṇamissā āpodhātu.
rows.english: [Spittle] 135. This is water element mixed with froth inside the mouth.

rows.pali: So vaṇṇato seto pheṇavaṇṇo.
rows.english: As to colour, it is white, the colour of the froth.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato okāsasaṇṭhāno.
rows.english: As to shape, it is the shape of its location,

rows.pali: Pheṇasaṇṭhānotipi vattuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: or it can be called “the shape of froth.”

rows.pali: Disato uparimāya disāya jāto.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to the upper direction.

rows.pali: Okāsato ubhohi kapolapassehi oruyha jivhāya ṭhito.
rows.english: As to location, it is to be found on the tongue after it has descended from the cheeks on both sides.

rows.pali: Na cesa ettha sadā sannicito hutvā tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: And it is not always to be found stored there;

rows.pali: Yadā pana sattā tathārūpamāhāraṃ passanti vā saranti vā, uṇhatittakaṭukaloṇambilānaṃ vā kiñci mukhe ṭhapenti, yadā vā nesaṃ hadayaṃ āgilāyati, kismiñci deva vā jigucchā uppajjati, tadā kheḷo uppajjitvā ubhohi kapolapassehi oruyha jivhāya saṇṭhāti.
rows.english: but when beings see particular kinds of food, or remember them, or put something hot or bitter or sharp or salty or sour into their mouths, or when their hearts are faint, or nausea arises on some account, then spittle appears and runs down from the cheeks on both sides to settle on the tongue.

rows.pali: Aggajivhāya cesa tanuko hoti, mūlajivhāya bahalo, mukhe pakkhittañca puthukaṃ vā taṇḍulaṃ vā aññaṃ vā kiñci khādanīyaṃ nadīpuline khatakūpakasalilaṃ viya parikkhayaṃ agacchantova temetuṃ samattho hoti.
rows.english: It is thin at the tip of the tongue, and thick at the root of the tongue. It is capable, without getting used up, of wetting unhusked rice or husked rice or anything else chewable that is put into the mouth, like the water in a pit scooped out in a river sand bank.

rows.pali: Paricchedato kheḷabhāgena paricchinno, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded by what appertains to spittle …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisova.
211.Siṅghāṇikāti matthaluṅgato paggharaṇakaasuci.
rows.english: [Snot] 136. This is impurity that trickles out from the brain.

rows.pali: Sā vaṇṇato taruṇatālaṭṭhimiñjavaṇṇā.
rows.english: As to colour, it is the colour of a young palmyra kernel.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato okāsasaṇṭhānā.
rows.english: As to shape, it is the shape of its location.

rows.pali: Disato uparimāya disāya jātā.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to the upper direction.

rows.pali: Okāsato nāsāpuṭe pūretvā ṭhitā.
rows.english: As to location, it is to be found filling the nostril cavities.

rows.pali: Na cesā ettha sadā sannicitā hutvā tiṭṭhati, atha kho yathā nāma puriso paduminipatte dadhiṃ bandhitvā heṭṭhā kaṇṭakena vijjheyya, athānena chiddena dadhimuttaṃ gaḷitvā bahi pateyya, evameva yadā sattā rodanti, visabhāgāhārautuvasena vā sañjātadhātukhobhā honti, tadā anto sīsato pūtisemhabhāvamāpannaṃ matthaluṅgaṃ gaḷitvā tālumatthakavivarena otaritvā nāsāpuṭe pūretvā tiṭṭhati vā paggharati vā.
rows.english: And it is not always to be found stored there; but rather, just as though a man tied up curd in a lotus leaf, which he then pricked with a thorn underneath, and whey oozed out and dripped, so too, when beings weep or suffer a disturbance of elements produced by wrong food or temperature, then the brain inside the head turns into stale phlegm, and it oozes out and comes down by an opening in the palate, and it fills the nostrils and stays there or trickles out.

rows.pali: Siṅghāṇikā pariggaṇhakena ca yoginā nāsāpuṭe pūretvā ṭhitavaseneva pariggaṇhitabbā.
rows.english: And the meditator who discerns snot should discern it only as it is to be found filling the nostril cavities.

rows.pali: Paricchedato siṅghāṇikābhāgena paricchinnā, ayamassā sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded by what appertains to snot …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisova.
212.Lasikāti sarīrasandhīnaṃ abbhantare picchilakuṇapaṃ.
rows.english: [Oil of the Joints] 137. This is the slimy ordure inside the joints in the body.

rows.pali: Sā vaṇṇato kaṇikāraniyyāsavaṇṇā.
rows.english: As to colour, it is the colour of kaṇikāra gum.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato okāsasaṇṭhānā.
rows.english: As to shape, it is the shape of its location.

rows.pali: Disato dvīsu disāsu jātā.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to both directions.

rows.pali: Okāsato aṭṭhisandhīnaṃ abbhañjanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā asītisatasandhīnaṃ abbhantare ṭhitā.
rows.english: As to location, it is to be found inside the hundred and eighty joints, serving the function of lubricating the bones’ joints.

rows.pali: Yassa cesā mandā hoti, tassa uṭṭhahantassa nisīdantassa abhikkamantassa paṭikkamantassa samiñjantassa pasārentassa aṭṭhikāni kaṭakaṭāyanti, accharāsaddaṃ karonto viya sañcarati.
rows.english: If it is weak, when a man gets up or sits down, moves forward or backward, bends or stretches, then his bones creak, and he goes about making a noise like the snapping of fingers,

rows.pali: Ekayojanadviyojanamattaṃ addhānaṃ gatassa vāyodhātu kuppati, gattāni dukkhanti.
rows.english: and when he has walked only one or two leagues’ distance, his air element gets upset and his limbs pain him.

rows.pali: Yassa pana bahukā honti, tassa uṭṭhānanisajjādīsu na aṭṭhīni kaṭakaṭāyanti, dīghampi addhānaṃ gatassa na vāyodhātu kuppati, na gattāni dukkhanti.
rows.english: But if a man has plenty of it, his bones do not creak when he gets up, sits down, etc., and even when he has walked a long distance, his air element does not get upset and his limbs do not pain him.

rows.pali: Paricchedato lasikābhāgena paricchinnā, ayamassā sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is bounded by what appertains to oil of the joints …

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisova.
213.Muttanti muttarasaṃ.
rows.english: [Urine] 138. This is the urine solution.

rows.pali: Taṃ vaṇṇato māsakhārodakavaṇṇaṃ.
rows.english: As to colour, it is the colour of bean brine.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānato adhomukhaṭṭhapitaudakakumbhaabbhantaragataudakasaṇṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: As to shape, it is the shape of water inside a water pot placed upside down.

rows.pali: Disato heṭṭhimāya disāya jātaṃ.
rows.english: As to direction, it belongs to the lower direction.

rows.pali: Okāsato vatthissa abbhantare ṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: As to location, it is to be found inside the bladder.

rows.pali: Vatthi nāma vatthi puṭo vuccati.
rows.english: For the bladder sack is called the bladder.

rows.pali: Yattha seyyathāpi candanikāya pakkhitte amukhe ravaṇaghaṭe candanikāraso pavisati, na cassa pavisanamaggo paññāyati, evameva sarīrato muttaṃ pavisati, na cassa pavisanamaggo paññāyati, nikkhamanamaggo pana pākaṭo hoti.
rows.english: Just as when a porous pot with no mouth is put into a cesspool, then the solution from the cesspool gets into the porous pot with no mouth even though no way of entry is evident, so too, while the urinary secretion from the body enters the bladder its way of entry is not evident. Its way of exit, however, is evident.

rows.pali: Yamhi ca muttassa bharite passāvaṃ karomāti sattānaṃ āyūhanaṃ hoti.
rows.english: And when the bladder is full of urine, beings feel the need to make water.

rows.pali: Paricchedato vatthiabbhantarena ceva muttabhāgena ca paricchinnaṃ, ayamassa sabhāgaparicchedo.
rows.english: As to delimitation, it is delimited by the inside of the bladder and by what is similar to urine. This is the delimitation by the similar.

rows.pali: Visabhāgaparicchedo pana kesasadisova.
rows.english: But its delimitation by the dissimilar is like that for the head hairs (see note at end of §90).

rows.pali: 214.Evañhi kesādike koṭṭhāse vaṇṇasaṇṭhānadisokāsaparicchedavasena vavatthapetvā anupubbato nātisīghatotiādinā nayena vaṇṇasaṇṭhānagandhāsayokāsavasena pañcadhā paṭikkūlā paṭikkūlāti manasikaroto paṇṇattisamatikkamāvasāne seyyathāpi cakkhumato purisassa dvattiṃsavaṇṇānaṃ kusumānaṃ ekasuttakaganthitaṃ mālaṃ olokentassa sabbapupphāni apubbāpariyamiva pākaṭāni honti, evameva atthi imasmiṃ kāye kesāti imaṃ kāyaṃ olokentassa sabbe te dhammā apubbāpariyāva pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: [The Arising of Absorption] 139. When the meditator has defined the parts beginning with the head hairs in this way by colour, shape, direction, location and delimitation (§58), and he gives his attention in the ways beginning with “following the order, not too quickly” (§61) to their repulsiveness in the five aspects of colour, shape, smell, habitat, and location (§84f.), then at last he surmounts the concept (§66). Then just as when a man with good sight is observing a garland of flowers of thirty- two colours knotted on a single string and all the flowers become evident to him simultaneously, so too, when the meditator observes this body thus, “There are in this body head hairs,” then all these things become evident to him, as it were, simultaneously.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ manasikārakosallakathāyaṃ "ādikammikassa hi kesāti manasikaroto manasikāro gantvā muttanti imaṃ pariyosānakoṭṭhāsameva āhacca tiṭṭhatī"ti.
rows.english: Hence it was said above in the explanation of skill in giving attention: “For when a beginner gives his attention to head hairs, his attention carries on till it arrives at the last part, that is, urine, and stops there” (§67).

rows.pali: Sace pana bahiddhāpi manasikāraṃ upasaṃharati, athassa evaṃ sabbakoṭṭhāsesu pākaṭībhūtesu āhiṇḍantā manussatiracchānādayo sattākāraṃ vijahitvā koṭṭhāsarāsivaseneva upaṭṭhahanti, tehi ca ajjhohariyamānaṃ pānabhojanādi koṭṭhāsarāsimhi pakkhipamānamiva upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: 140. If he applies his attention externally as well when all the parts have become evident in this way, then human beings, animals, etc., as they go about are divested of their aspect of beings and appear as just assemblages of parts. And when drink, food, etc., is being swallowed by them, it appears as though it were being put in among the assemblage of parts.

rows.pali: Athassa anupubbamuñcanādivasena paṭikkūlā paṭikkūlāti punappunaṃ manasikaroto anukkamena appanā uppajjati.
rows.english: 141. Then, as he gives his attention to them again and again as “Repulsive, repulsive,” employing the process of “successive leaving,” etc. (§67), eventually absorption arises in him.

rows.pali: Tattha kesādīnaṃ vaṇṇasaṇṭhānadisokāsaparicchedavasena upaṭṭhānaṃ uggahanimittaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, the appearance of the head hairs, etc., as to colour, shape, direction, location, and delimitation is the learning sign;

rows.pali: Sabbākārato paṭikkūlavasena upaṭṭhānaṃ paṭibhāganimittaṃ.
rows.english: their appearance as repulsive in all aspects is the counterpart sign.

rows.pali: Taṃ āsevato bhāvayato vuttanayena asubhakammaṭṭhānesu viya paṭhamajjhānavaseneva appanā uppajjati.
rows.english: As he cultivates and develops that counterpart sign, absorption arises in him, but only of the first jhāna, in the same way as described under foulness as a meditation subject (VI.64f.).

rows.pali: Sā yassa ekova koṭṭhāso pākaṭo hoti, ekasmiṃ vā koṭṭhāse appanaṃ patvā puna aññasmiṃ yogaṃ na karoti, tassa ekāva uppajjati.
rows.english: And it arises singly in one to whom only one part has become evident, or who has reached absorption in one part and makes no further effort about another.

rows.pali: Yassa pana aneke koṭṭhāsā pākaṭā honti, ekasmiṃ vā jhānaṃ patvā puna aññasmiṃpi yogaṃ karoti, tassa mallakattherassa viya koṭṭhāsagaṇanāya paṭhamajjhānāni nibbattanti.
rows.english: 142. But several first jhānas, according to the number of parts, are produced in one to whom several parts have become evident, or who has reached jhāna in one and also makes further effort about another. As in the case of the Elder Mallaka.

rows.pali: So kirāyasmā dīghabhāṇakaabhayattheraṃ hatthe gahetvā "āvuso abhaya, imaṃ tāva pañhaṃ uggaṇhāhī"ti vatvā āha – "mallakatthero dvattiṃsakoṭṭhāsesu dvattiṃsāya paṭhamajjhānānaṃ lābhī.
rows.english: The elder, it seems, took the Elder Abhaya, the Dīgha reciter, by the hand,36 and after saying “Friend Abhaya, first learn this matter,” he went on: “The Elder Mallaka is an obtainer of thirty-two jhānas in the thirty-two parts.

rows.pali: Sace rattiṃ ekaṃ, divā ekaṃ samāpajjati, atirekaddhamāsena puna sampajjati, sace pana devasikaṃ ekaṃ samāpajjati, atirekamāsena puna sampajjatī"ti.
rows.english: If he enters upon one by night and one by day, he goes on entering upon them for over a fortnight; but if he enters upon one each day, he goes on entering upon them for over a month.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ paṭhamajjhānavasena ijjhamānampi cetaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ vaṇṇasaṇṭhānādīsu satibalena ijjhanato kāyagatāsatīti vuccati.
rows.english: 143. And although this meditation is successful in this way with the first jhāna, it is nevertheless called “mindfulness occupied with the body” because it is successful through the influence of the mindfulness of the colour, shape, and so on.

rows.pali: Imañca kāyagatāsatimanuyutto bhikkhu aratiratisaho hoti, na ca naṃ arati sahati, uppannaṃ aratiṃ abhibhuyya abhibhuyya viharati.
rows.english: 144. And the bhikkhu who is devoted to this mindfulness occupied with the body “is a conqueror of boredom and delight, and boredom does not conquer him; he dwells transcending boredom as it arises.

rows.pali: Bhayabheravasaho hoti, na ca naṃ bhayabheravaṃ sahati, uppannaṃ bhayabheravaṃ abhibhuyya abhibhuyya viharati.
rows.english: He is a conqueror of fear and dread, and fear and dread do not conquer him; he dwells transcending fear and dread as they arise.

rows.pali: Khamo hoti sītassa uṇhassa - pe - pāṇaharānaṃ adhivāsakajātiko hoti (ma. ni. 3.159).
rows.english: He is one who bears cold and heat … who endures … arisen bodily feelings that are … menacing to life” (M III 97);

rows.pali: Kesādīnaṃ vaṇṇabhedaṃ nissāya catunnaṃ jhānānaṃ lābhī hoti.
rows.english: he becomes an obtainer of the four jhānas based on the colour aspect of the head hairs,37 etc.;

rows.pali: Cha abhiññā paṭivijjhati (ma. ni. 3.159).
rows.english: and he comes to penetrate the six kinds of direct-knowledge (see MN 6).

rows.pali: Idaṃ kāyagatāsatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with mindfulness occupied with the body in the detailed treatise.

rows.pali: 215.Idāni yaṃ taṃ bhagavatā "ayampi kho, bhikkhave, ānāpānassatisamādhi bhāvito bahulīkato santo ceva paṇīto ca asecanako ca sukho ca vihāro, uppannuppanne ca pāpake akusale dhamme ṭhānaso antaradhāpeti vūpasametī"ti evaṃ pasaṃsitvā –
rows.english: It has been recommended by the Blessed One thus: “And, bhikkhus, this concentration through mindfulness of breathing, when developed and practiced much, is both peaceful and sublime, it is an unadulterated blissful abiding, and it banishes at once and stills evil unprofitable thoughts as soon as they arise” (S V 321; Vin III 70).

rows.pali: Evaṃ soḷasavatthukaṃ ānāpānassatikammaṭṭhānaṃ niddiṭṭhaṃ.
Tassa bhāvanānayo anuppatto.
rows.english: 146. The description [of development] is complete in all respects,

rows.pali: So pana yasmā pāḷivaṇṇanānusāreneva vuccamāno sabbākāraparipūro hoti.
rows.english: however, only if it is given in due course after a commentary on the text.

rows.pali: Tasmā ayamettha pāḷivaṇṇanāpubbaṅgamo niddeso.
rows.english: So it is given here (§186) introduced by a commentary on the [first part of the] text.

rows.pali: 216.Kathaṃ bhāvito ca, bhikkhave, ānāpānassati samādhīti ettha tāva kathanti ānāpānassatisamādhibhāvanaṃ nānappakārato vitthāretukamyatāpucchā.
rows.english: And how developed, bhikkhus, how practiced much, is concentration through mindfulness of breathing: here in the first place how is a question showing desire to explain in detail the development of concentration through mindfulness of breathing in its various forms.

rows.pali: Bhāvito ca bhikkhave ānāpānassatisamādhīti nānappakārato vitthāretukamyatāya puṭṭhadhammanidassanaṃ.
rows.english: Developed, bhikkhus, … is concentration through mindfulness of breathing: this shows the thing that is being asked about out of desire to explain it in its various forms.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ bahulīkato - pe - vūpasametīti etthāpi eseva nayo.
rows.english: How practiced much … as soon as they arise?: here too the same explanation applies.

rows.pali: Tattha bhāvitoti uppādito vaḍḍhito vā.
rows.english: 147. Herein, developed means aroused or increased,

rows.pali: Ānāpānassatisamādhīti ānāpānapariggāhikāya satiyā saddhiṃ sampayutto samādhi.
rows.english: concentration through mindfulness of breathing (lit. “breathing-mindfulness concentration”) is either concentration associated with mindfulness that discerns breathing,

rows.pali: Ānāpānassatiyaṃ vā samādhi ānāpānassatisamādhi.
rows.english: or it is concentration on mindfulness of breathing.

rows.pali: Bahulīkatoti punappunaṃ kato.
rows.english: Practiced much: practiced again and again.

rows.pali: Santoceva paṇīto cāti santo ceva paṇīto ceva.
rows.english: 148. Both peaceful and sublime (santo c’ eva paṇīto ca): it is peaceful in both ways and sublime in both ways;

rows.pali: Ubhayattha eva saddena niyamo veditabbo.
rows.english: the two words should each be understood as governed by the word “both” (eva).

rows.pali: Kiṃ vuttaṃ hoti?
rows.english: What is meant?

rows.pali: Ayañhi yathā asubhakammaṭṭhānaṃ kevalaṃ paṭivedhavasena santañca paṇītañca, oḷārikārammaṇattā pana paṭikkūlārammaṇattā ca ārammaṇavasena neva santaṃ na paṇītaṃ, na evaṃ kenaci pariyāyena asanto vā apaṇīto vā, atha kho ārammaṇasantatāyapi santo vūpasanto nibbuto, paṭivedhasaṅkhātaaṅgasantatāyapi.
rows.english: Unlike foulness, which as a meditation subject is peaceful and sublime only by penetration, but is neither (n’ eva) peaceful nor sublime in its object since its object [in the learning stage] is gross, and [after that] its object is repulsiveness—unlike that, this is not unpeaceful or unsublime in any way, but on the contrary it is peaceful, stilled and quiet both on account of the peacefulness of its object and on account of the peacefulness of that one of its factors called penetration.

rows.pali: Ārammaṇapaṇītatāyapi paṇīto atittikaro, aṅgapaṇītatāyapīti.
rows.english: And it is sublime, something one cannot have enough of, both on account of the sublimeness of its object and on [268] account of the sublimeness of the aforesaid factor.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "santo ceva paṇīto cā"ti.
rows.english: Hence it is called “both peaceful and sublime.”

rows.pali: Asecanako ca sukho ca vihāroti ettha pana nāssa secananti asecanako, anāsittako abbokiṇṇo pāṭiyekko āveṇiko.
rows.english: 149. It is an unadulterated blissful abiding: it has no adulteration, thus it is unadulterated; it is unalloyed, unmixed, particular, special.

rows.pali: Natthi ettha parikammena vā upacārena vā santatā.
rows.english: Here it is not a question of peacefulness to be reached through preliminary work [as with the kasiṇas] or through access [as with foulness, for instance].

rows.pali: Ādisamannāhārato pabhuti attano sabhāveneva santo ca paṇīto cāti attho.
rows.english: It is peaceful and sublime in its own individual essence too starting with the very first attention given to it.

rows.pali: Keci pana asecanakoti anāsittako ojavanto sabhāveneva madhuroti vadanti.
rows.english: But some38 say that it is “unadulterated” because it is unalloyed, possessed of nutritive value and sweet in its individual essence too.

rows.pali: Evaṃ ayaṃ asecanako ca, appitappitakkhaṇe kāyikacetasikasukhapaṭilābhāya saṃvattanato sukho ca vihāroti veditabbo.
rows.english: So it should be understood to be “unadulterated” and a “blissful abiding” since it leads to the obtaining of bodily and mental bliss with every moment of absorption.

rows.pali: Uppannuppanneti avikkhambhite avikkhambhite.
rows.english: 150. As soon as they arise: whenever they are not suppressed.

rows.pali: Pāpaketi lāmake.
rows.english: Evil: bad.

rows.pali: Akusale dhammeti akosallasambhūte dhamme.
rows.english: Unprofitable (akusala) thoughts: thoughts produced by unskilfulness (akosalla).

rows.pali: Ṭhānaso antaradhāpetīti khaṇeneva antaradhāpeti vikkhambheti.
rows.english: It banishes at once: it banishes, suppresses, at that very moment.

rows.pali: Vūpasametīti suṭṭhu upasameti.
rows.english: Stills (vūpasameti): it thoroughly calms (suṭṭhu upasameti);

rows.pali: Nibbedhabhāgiyattā vā anupubbena ariyamaggavuddhippatto samucchindati, paṭippassambhetīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: or else, when eventually brought to fulfilment by the noble path, it cuts off, because of partaking of penetration; it tranquilizes, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panettha saṅkhepattho.
rows.english: 151. In brief, however, the meaning here is this:

rows.pali: Bhikkhave, kena pakārena kenākārena kena vidhinā bhāvito ānāpānassatisamādhi kena pakārena bahulīkato santo ceva - pe - vūpasametīti.
rows.english: “Bhikkhus, in what way, in what manner, by what system, is concentration through mindfulness of breathing developed, in what way is it practiced much, that it is both peaceful … as soon as they arise?”

rows.pali: 217.Idāni tamatthaṃ vitthārento "idha, bhikkhave"tiādimāha.
rows.english: 152. He now said, “Here, bhikkhus,” etc., giving the meaning of that in detail.

rows.pali: Tattha idha bhikkhave bhikkhūti bhikkhave, imasmiṃ sāsane bhikkhu.
rows.english: Herein, here, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu means: bhikkhus, in this dispensation a bhikkhu.

rows.pali: Ayañhi ettha idhasaddo sabbappakāraānāpānassatisamādhinibbattakassa puggalassa sannissayabhūtasāsanaparidīpano aññasāsanassa tathābhāvapaṭisedhano ca.
rows.english: For this word here signifies the [Buddha’s] dispensation as the prerequisite for a person to produce concentration through mindfulness of breathing in all its modes,39 and it denies that such a state exists in any other dispensation.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ – idheva, bhikkhave, samaṇo - pe - suññā parappavādā samaṇebhi aññehī"ti (ma. ni. 1.139).
rows.english: For this is said: “Bhikkhus, only here is there an ascetic, here a second ascetic, here a third ascetic, here a fourth ascetic; other dispensations are devoid of ascetics” (M I 63; A II 238).40

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "imasmiṃ sāsane bhikkhū"ti.
rows.english: That is why it was said above “in this dispensation a bhikkhu.”

rows.pali: Araññagato vā - pe - suññāgāragato vāti idamassa ānāpānassatisamādhibhāvanānurūpasenāsanapariggahaparidīpanaṃ.
rows.english: 153. Gone to the forest … or to an empty place: this signifies that he has found an abode favourable to the development of concentration through mindfulness of breathing.

rows.pali: Imassa hi bhikkhuno dīgharattaṃ rūpādīsu ārammaṇesu anuvisaṭaṃ cittaṃ ānāpānassatisamādhiārammaṇaṃ abhiruhituṃ na icchati, kūṭagoṇayuttaratho viya uppathameva dhāvati.
rows.english: For this bhikkhu’s mind has long been dissipated among visible data, etc., as its object, and it does not want to mount the object of concentration- through-mindfulness-of-breathing; it runs off the track like a chariot harnessed to a wild ox.41

rows.pali: Tasmā seyyathāpi nāma gopo kūṭadhenuyā sabbaṃ khīraṃ pivitvā vaḍḍhitaṃ kūṭavacchaṃ dametukāmo dhenuto apanetvā ekamante mahantaṃ thambhaṃ nikhaṇitvā tattha yottena bandheyya, athassa so vaccho ito cito ca vipphanditvā palāyituṃ asakkonto tameva thambhaṃ upanisīdeyya vā upanipajjeyya vā, evameva imināpi bhikkhunā dīgharattaṃ rūpārammaṇādirasapānavaḍḍhitaṃ duṭṭhacittaṃ dametukāmena rūpādiārammaṇato apanetvā araññaṃ vā - pe - suññāgāraṃ vā pavesetvā tattha assāsapassāsathambhe satiyottena bandhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Now, suppose a cowherd [269] wanted to tame a wild calf that had been reared on a wild cow’s milk, he would take it away from the cow and tie it up apart with a rope to a stout post dug into the ground; then the calf might dash to and fro, but being unable to get away, it would eventually sit down or lie down by the post. So too, when a bhikkhu wants to tame his own mind which has long been spoilt by being reared on visible data, etc., as object for its food and drink, he should take it away from visible data, etc., as object and bring it into the forest or to the root of a tree or to an empty place and tie it up there to the post of in-breaths and out-breaths with the rope of mindfulness.

rows.pali: Evamassa taṃ cittaṃ ito cito ca vipphanditvāpi pubbe āciṇṇārammaṇaṃ alabhamānaṃ satiyottaṃ chinditvā palāyituṃ asakkontaṃ tamevārammaṇaṃ upacārappanāvasena upanisīdati ceva upanipajjati ca.
rows.english: And so his mind may then dash to and fro when it no longer gets the objects it was formerly used to, but being unable to break the rope of mindfulness and get away, it sits down, lies down, by that object under the influence of access and absorption.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: Evamassetaṃ senāsanaṃ bhāvanānurūpaṃ hoti.
rows.english: This is how an abode is favourable to his development.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "idamassa ānāpānassatisamādhibhāvanānurūpasenāsanapariggahaparidīpana"nti.
rows.english: Hence it was said above: “This signifies that he has found an abode favourable to the development of concentration through mindfulness of breathing. ”

rows.pali: Atha vā yasmā idaṃ kammaṭṭhānappabhede muddhabhūtaṃ sabbaññubuddhapaccekabuddhabuddhasāvakānaṃ visesādhigamadiṭṭhadhammasukhavihārapadaṭṭhānaṃ ānāpānassatikammaṭṭhānaṃ itthipurisahatthiassādisaddasamākulaṃ gāmantaṃ apariccajitvā na sukaraṃ bhāvetuṃ, saddakaṇṭakattā jhānassa.
rows.english: 155.Or alternatively, this mindfulness of breathing as a meditation subject— which is foremost among the various meditation subjects of all Buddhas, [some] Paccekabuddhas and [some] Buddhas’ disciples as a basis for attaining distinction and abiding in bliss here and now—is not easy to develop without leaving the neighbourhood of villages, which resound with the noises of women, men, elephants, horses, etc., noise being a thorn to jhāna (see A V 135),

rows.pali: Agāmake pana araññe sukaraṃ yogāvacarena idaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ pariggahetvā ānāpānacatutthajjhānaṃ nibbattetvā tadeva pādakaṃ katvā saṅkhāre sammasitvā aggaphalaṃ arahattaṃ sampāpuṇituṃ.
rows.english: whereas in the forest away from a village a meditator can at his ease set about discerning this meditation subject and achieve the fourth jhāna in mindfulness of breathing; and then, by making that same jhāna the basis for comprehension of formations [with insight] (XX.2f.), he can reach Arahantship, the highest fruit.

rows.pali: Tasmāssa anurūpasenāsanaṃ dassento bhagavā "araññagato vā"tiādimāha.
rows.english: That is why the Blessed One said “gone to the forest,” etc., in pointing out a favourable abode for him.

rows.pali: Vatthuvijjācariyo viya hi bhagavā, so yathā vatthuvijjācariyo nagarabhūmiṃ passitvā suṭṭhu upaparikkhitvā "ettha nagaraṃ māpethā"ti upadisati, sotthinā ca nagare niṭṭhite rājakulato mahāsakkāraṃ labhati, evameva yogāvacarassa anurūpasenāsanaṃ upaparikkhitvā "ettha kammaṭṭhānaṃ anuyuñjitabba"nti upadisati, tato tattha kammaṭṭhānaṃ anuyuttena yoginā kamena arahatte patte "sammāsambuddho vata so bhagavā"ti mahantaṃ sakkāraṃ labhati.
rows.english: 156. For the Blessed One is like a master of the art of building sites (see D I 9, 12; II 87). [270] As the master of the art of building sites surveys the proposed site for a town, thoroughly examines it, and then gives his directions, “Build the town here,” and when the town is safely finished, he receives great honour from the royal family, so the Blessed One examines an abode as to its suitability for the meditator, and he directs, “Devote yourself to the meditation subject here,” and later on, when the meditator has devoted himself to the meditation subject and has reached Arahantship and says, “The Blessed One is indeed fully enlightened,” the Blessed One receives great honour.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana bhikkhu dīpisadisoti vuccati.
rows.english: 157. And this bhikkhu is compared to a leopard.

rows.pali: Yathā hi mahādīpirājā araññe tiṇagahanaṃ vā vanagahanaṃ vā pabbatagahanaṃ vā nissāya nilīyitvā vanamahiṃsagokaṇṇasūkarādayo mige gaṇhāti, evameva ayaṃ araññādīsu kammaṭṭhānaṃ anuyuñjanto bhikkhu yathākkamena sotāpattisakadāgāmianāgāmiarahattamagge ceva ariyaphalañca gaṇhatīti veditabbo.
rows.english: For just as a great leopard king lurks in a grass wilderness or a jungle wilderness or a rock wilderness in the forest and seizes wild beasts—the wild buffalo, wild ox, boar, etc.—so too, the bhikkhu who devotes himself to his meditation subject in the forest, etc., should be understood to seize successively the paths of stream-entry, once-return, non-return, and Arahantship; and the noble fruitions as well.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: Tenassa parakkamajavayoggabhūmiṃ araññasenāsanaṃ dassento bhagavā "araññagato vā"tiādimāha.
rows.english: So the Blessed One said “gone to the forest,” etc., to point out a forest abode as a place likely to hasten his advancement.

rows.pali: 218.Tattha araññagatoti "araññanti nikkhamitvā bahi indakhīlā sabbametaṃ arañña"nti (vibha. 529) ca, "āraññakaṃ nāma senāsanaṃ pañcadhanusatikaṃ pacchima"nti (pārā. 654) ca evaṃ vuttalakkhaṇesu araññesu yaṃkiñci pavivekasukhaṃ araññaṃ gato.
rows.english: 158. Herein, gone to the forest is gone to any kind of forest possessing the bliss of seclusion among the kinds of forests characterized thus: “Having gone out beyond the boundary post, all that is forest” (Paṭis I 176; Vibh 251), and “A forest abode is five hundred bow lengths distant” (Vin IV 183).

rows.pali: Rukkhamūlagatoti rukkhasamīpaṃ gato.
rows.english: To the root of a tree: gone to the vicinity of a tree.

rows.pali: Suññāgāragatoti suññaṃ vivittokāsaṃ gato.
rows.english: To an empty place: gone to an empty, secluded space.

rows.pali: Ettha ca ṭhapetvā araññañca rukkhamūlañca avasesasattavidhasenāsanagatopi suññāgāragatoti vattuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: And here he can be said to have gone to an “empty place” if he has gone to any of the remaining seven kinds of abode (resting place). 42 [271]

rows.pali: Evamassa ututtayānukūlaṃ dhātucariyānukūlañca ānāpānassatibhāvanānurūpaṃ senāsanaṃ upadisitvā alīnānuddhaccapakkhikaṃ santaṃ iriyāpathaṃ upadisanto nisīdatīti āha.
rows.english: 159. Having thus indicated an abode that is suitable to the three seasons, suitable to humour and temperament,43 and favourable to the development of mindfulness of breathing, he then said sits down, etc., indicating a posture that is peaceful and tends neither to idleness nor to agitation.

rows.pali: Athassa nisajjāya daḷhabhāvaṃ assāsapassāsānaṃ pavattanasukhataṃ ārammaṇapariggahūpāyañca dassento pallaṅkaṃ ābhujitvātiādimāha.
rows.english: Then he said having folded his legs crosswise, etc., to show firmness in the sitting position, easy occurrence of the in-breaths and out-breaths, and the means for discerning the object.

rows.pali: Tattha pallaṅkanti samantato ūrubaddhāsanaṃ.
rows.english: 160. Herein, crosswise is the sitting position with the thighs fully locked.

rows.pali: Ābhujitvāti bandhitvā.
rows.english: Folded: having locked.

rows.pali: Ujuṃ kāyaṃ paṇidhāyāti uparimasarīraṃ ujukaṃ ṭhapetvā.
rows.english: Set his body erect: having placed the upper part of the body erect

rows.pali: Aṭṭhārasapiṭṭhikaṇṭake koṭiyā koṭiṃ paṭipādetvā.
rows.english: with the eighteen backbones resting end to end.

rows.pali: Evañhi nisīdantassa cammamaṃsanhārūni na paṇamanti.
rows.english: For when he is seated like this, his skin, flesh and sinews are not twisted,

rows.pali: Athassa yā tesaṃ paṇamanappaccayā khaṇe khaṇe vedanā uppajjeyyuṃ, tā na uppajjanti.
rows.english: and so the feelings that would arise moment by moment if they were twisted do not arise.

rows.pali: Tāsu anuppajjamānāsu cittaṃ ekaggaṃ hoti, kammaṭṭhānaṃ na paripatati, vuddhiṃ phātiṃ upagacchati.
rows.english: That being so, his mind becomes unified, and the meditation subject, instead of collapsing, attains to growth and increase.

rows.pali: Parimukhaṃ satiṃ upaṭṭhapetvāti kammaṭṭhānābhimukhaṃ satiṃ ṭhapayitvā.
rows.english: 161. Established mindfulness in front of him (parimukhaṃ satiṃ upaṭṭhapetvā) = having placed (ṭhapayitvā) mindfulness (satiṃ) facing the meditation subject (kammaṭṭhānābhimukhaṃ).

rows.pali: Atha vā parīti pariggahaṭṭho.
rows.english: Or alternatively, the meaning can be treated here too according to the method of explanation given in the Paṭisambhidā, which is this: Pari has the sense of control (pariggaha),

rows.pali: Mukhanti niyyānaṭṭho.
rows.english: mukhaṃ (lit. mouth) has the sense of outlet (niyyāna),

rows.pali: Satīti upaṭṭhānaṭṭho.
rows.english: sati has the sense of establishment (upaṭṭhāna);

rows.pali: Tena vuccati "parimukhaṃ sati"nti evaṃ paṭisambhidāyaṃ (paṭi. ma. 1.164) vuttanayenapettha attho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: that is why parimukhaṃ satiṃ (‘mindfulness as a controlled outlet’) is said” (Paṭis I 176).

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ saṅkhepo, pariggahitaniyyānaṃ satiṃ katvāti.
rows.english: The meaning of it in brief is: Having made mindfulness the outlet (from opposition, forgetfulness being thereby] controlled.44

rows.pali: 219.Sosatova assasati sato passasatīti so bhikkhu evaṃ nisīditvā evañca satiṃ upaṭṭhapetvā taṃ satiṃ avijahanto sato eva assasati sato passasati, satokārī hotīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 162. Ever mindful he breathes in, mindful he breathes out: having seated himself thus, having established mindfulness thus, the bhikkhu does not abandon that mindfulness; ever mindful he breathes in, mindful he breathes out; he is a mindful worker, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Idāni yehākārehi satokārī hoti, te dassetuṃ dīghaṃ vā assasantotiādimāha.
rows.english: 163.(i) Now, breathing in long, etc., is said in order to show the different ways in which he is a mindful worker.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ paṭisambhidāyaṃ "so satova assasati sato passasatī"ti etasseva vibhaṅge –
rows.english: For in the Paṭisambhidā, in the exposition of the clause, “Ever mindful he breathes in, mindful he breathes out,” this is said:

rows.pali: Tattha dīghaṃ vā assasantoti dīghaṃ vā assāsaṃ pavattayanto.
rows.english: 164. Herein, breathing in long (assasanto) is producing a long in-breath.[272]

rows.pali: Assāsoti bahi nikkhamanavāto.
rows.english: “Assāsa is the wind issuing out;

rows.pali: Passāsoti anto pavisanavātoti vinayaṭṭhakathāyaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: passāsa is the wind entering in” is said in the Vinaya Commentary.

rows.pali: Suttantaṭṭhakathāsu pana uppaṭipāṭiyā āgataṃ.
rows.english: But in the Suttanta Commentaries it is given in the opposite sense.

rows.pali: Tattha sabbesampi gabbhaseyyakānaṃ mātukucchito nikkhamanakāle paṭhamaṃ abbhantaravāto bahi nikkhamati.
rows.english: Herein, when any infant comes out from the mother’s womb, first the wind from within goes out

rows.pali: Pacchā bāhiravāto sukhumarajaṃ gahetvā abbhantaraṃ pavisanto tāluṃ āhacca nibbāyati.
rows.english: and subsequently the wind from without enters in with fine dust, strikes the palate and is extinguished [with the infant’s sneezing].

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva assāsapassāsā veditabbā.
rows.english: This, firstly, is how assāsa and passāsa should be understood.

rows.pali: Yā pana tesaṃ dīgharassatā, sā addhānavasena veditabbā.
rows.english: 165. But their length and shortness should be understood by extent (addhāna).

rows.pali: Yathā hi okāsaddhānaṃ pharitvā ṭhitaṃ udakaṃ vā vālikā vā "dīghamudakaṃ dīghā vālikā, rassamudakaṃ rassā vālikā"ti vuccati, evaṃ cuṇṇavicuṇṇāpi assāsapassāsā hatthisarīre ca ahisarīre ca tesaṃ attabhāvasaṅkhātaṃ dīghaṃ addhānaṃ saṇikaṃ pūretvā saṇikameva nikkhamanti.
rows.english: For just as water or sand that occupies an extent of space is called a “long water,” a “long sand,” a “short water,” a “short sand,” so in the case of elephants’ and snakes’ bodies the in-breaths and out-breaths regarded as particles45 slowly fill the long extent, in other words, their persons, and slowly go out again.

rows.pali: Tasmā dīghāti vuccanti.
rows.english: That is why they are called “long.”

rows.pali: Sunakhasasādīnaṃ attabhāvasaṅkhātaṃ rassaṃ addhānaṃ sīghaṃ pūretvā sīghameva nikkhamanti, tasmā rassāti vuccanti.
rows.english: They rapidly fill a short extent, in other words, the person of a dog, a hare, etc., and rapidly go out again. That is why they are called “short. ”

rows.pali: Manussesu pana keci hatthiahiādayo viya kāladdhānavasena dīghaṃ assasanti ca passasanti ca.
rows.english: 166. And in the case of human beings some breathe in and breathe out long, by extent of time, as elephants, snakes, etc., do,

rows.pali: Keci sunakhasasādayo viya rassaṃ, tasmā tesaṃ kālavasena dīghamaddhānaṃ nikkhamantā ca pavisantā ca te "dīghā" ittaramaddhānaṃ nikkhamantā ca pavisantā ca "rassā"ti veditabbā.
rows.english: while others breathe in and breathe out short in that way as dogs, hares, etc., do. Of these, therefore, the breaths that travel over a long extent in entering in and going out are to be understood as long in time; and the breaths that travel over a little extent in entering in and going out, as short in time.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ bhikkhu navahākārehi dīghaṃ assasanto passasanto ca "dīghaṃ assasāmi, passasāmī"ti pajānāti.
rows.english: 167. Now, this bhikkhu knows “I breathe in, I breathe out, long” while breathing in and breathing out long in nine ways.

rows.pali: Evaṃ pajānato cassa ekenākārena kāyānupassanāsatipaṭṭhānabhāvanā sampajjatīti veditabbā.
rows.english: And the development of the foundation of mindfulness consisting in contemplation of the body should be understood to be perfected in one aspect in him who knows thus,

rows.pali: Yathāha paṭisambhidāyaṃ (paṭi. ma. 1.166) –
rows.english: according as it is said in the Paṭisambhidā:

rows.pali: Esa nayo rassapadepi.
rows.english: 169. (ii) The same method of explanation applies also in the case of short breaths.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana viseso, yathā ettha "dīghaṃ assāsaṃ addhānasaṅkhāte"ti vuttaṃ, evamidha "rassaṃ assāsaṃ ittarasaṅkhāte assasatī"ti āgataṃ.
rows.english: But there is this difference. While in the former case “a long in-breath reckoned as an extent” is said, here “a short in-breath reckoned as a little [duration]” (Paṭis I 182) is given.

rows.pali: Tasmā rassavasena yāva "tena vuccati kāye kāyānupassanāsatipaṭṭhānabhāvanā"ti, tāva yojetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So it must be construed as “short” as far as the phrase “That is why ‘development of the foundation (establishment) of mindfulness consisting in contemplation of the body as a body’ is said” (Paṭis I 183).

rows.pali: Evaṃ ayaṃ addhānavasena ittaravasena ca imehākārehi assāsapassāse pajānanto dīghaṃ vā assasanto dīghaṃ assasāmīti pajānāti - pe - rassaṃ vā passasanto rassaṃ passasāmīti pajānātīti veditabbo.
rows.english: 170. So it should be understood that it is when this bhikkhu knows in-breaths and out-breaths in these nine ways as “a [long] extent” and as “a little [duration]” that “breathing in long, he knows ‘I breathe in long;’ … breathing out short, he knows ‘I breathe out short’ is said of him.

rows.pali: Evaṃ pajānato cassa –
rows.english: And when he knows thus:

rows.pali: 220.Sabbakāyapaṭisaṃvedī assasissāmi - pe - passasissāmīti sikkhatīti sakalassa assāsakāyassa ādimajjhapariyosānaṃ viditaṃ karonto pākaṭaṃ karonto assasissāmīti sikkhati.
rows.english: 171.(iii) He trains thus: “I shall breathe in … I shall breathe out experiencing the whole body”: he trains thus: “I shall breathe in making known, making plain, the beginning, middle and end48 of the entire in-breath body.

rows.pali: Sakalassa passāsakāyassa ādimajjhapariyosānaṃ viditaṃ karonto pākaṭaṃ karonto passasissāmīti sikkhati.
rows.english: I shall breathe out making known, making plain, the beginning, middle and end of the entire out- breath body,” thus he trains.

rows.pali: Evaṃ viditaṃ karonto pākaṭaṃ karonto ñāṇasampayuttacittena assasati ceva passasati ca.
rows.english: Making them known, making them plain, in this way he both breathes in and breathes out with consciousness associated with knowledge.

rows.pali: Tasmā "assasissāmi passasissāmī"ti sikkhatīti vuccati.
rows.english: That is why it is said, “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out …’”

rows.pali: Ekassa hi bhikkhuno cuṇṇavicuṇṇavisaṭe assāsakāye passāsakāye vā ādi pākaṭo hoti, na majjhapariyosānaṃ.
rows.english: 172. To one bhikkhu the beginning of the in-breath body or the out-breath body, distributed in particles, [that is to say, regarded as successive arisings (see note 45)] is plain, but not the middle or the end;

rows.pali: So ādimeva pariggahetuṃ sakkoti, majjhapariyosāne kilamati.
rows.english: he is only able to discern the beginning and has difficulty with the middle and the end.

rows.pali: Ekassa majjhaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, na ādipariyosānaṃ.
rows.english: To another the middle is plain, not the beginning or the end; he is only able to discern the middle and has difficulty with the beginning and the end.

rows.pali: Ekassa pariyosānaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, na ādimajjhaṃ.
rows.english: To another the end is plain, not the beginning or the middle;

rows.pali: So pariyosānaṃyeva pariggahetuṃ sakkoti, ādimajjhe kilamati.
rows.english: he is only able to discern the end [274] and has difficulty with the beginning and the middle.

rows.pali: Ekassa sabbampi pākaṭaṃ hoti, so sabbampi pariggahetuṃ sakkoti, na katthaci kilamati, tādisena bhavitabbanti dassento āha – "sabbakāyapaṭisaṃvedī assasissāmīti - pe - passasissāmīti sikkhatī"ti.
rows.english: To yet another all stages are plain; he is able to discern them all and has no difficulty with any of them. Pointing out that one should be like the last-mentioned bhikkhu, he said: “He trains thus: ‘I shall breathe in … shall breathe out experiencing the whole body. ’”

rows.pali: Tattha sikkhatīti evaṃ ghaṭati vāyamati.
rows.english: 173. Herein, he trains: he strives, he endeavours in this way.

rows.pali: Yo vā tathābhūtassa saṃvaro, ayamettha adhisīlasikkhā.
rows.english: Or else the restraint here in one such as this is training in the higher virtue,

rows.pali: Yo tathābhūtassa samādhi, ayaṃ adhicittasikkhā.
rows.english: his consciousness is training in the higher consciousness,

rows.pali: Yā tathābhūtassa paññā, ayaṃ adhipaññāsikkhāti imā tisso sikkhāyo tasmiṃ ārammaṇe tāya satiyā tena manasikārena sikkhati āsevati bhāveti bahulīkarotīti evamettha attho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: and his understanding is training in the higher understanding (see Paṭis I 184). So he trains in, repeats, develops, repeatedly practices, these three kinds of training, on that object, by means of that mindfulness, by means of that attention. This is how the meaning should be regarded here.

rows.pali: Tattha yasmā purimanaye kevalaṃ assasitabbaṃ passasitabbameva, na ca aññaṃ kiñci kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 174. Herein, in the first part of the system (nos. i and ii)49 he should only breathe in and breathe out and not do anything else at all, and it is only afterwards that he should apply himself to the arousing of knowledge, and so on.

rows.pali: Ito paṭṭhāya pana ñāṇuppādanādīsu yogo karaṇīyo.
rows.english: Consequently the present tense is used here in the text, “He knows: ‘I breathe in’ … he knows: ‘I breathe out.’”

rows.pali: Tasmā tattha assasāmīti pajānāti passasāmīti pajānāticceva vattamānakālavasena pāḷiṃ vatvā ito paṭṭhāya kattabbassa ñāṇuppādanādino ākārassa dassanatthaṃ sabbakāyapaṭisaṃvedī assasissāmītiādinā nayena anāgatavacanavasena pāḷi āropitāti veditabbā.
rows.english: But the future tense in the passage beginning “I shall breathe in experiencing the whole body” should be understood as used in order to show that the aspect of arousing knowledge, etc., has to be undertaken from then on.

rows.pali: Passambhayaṃkāyasaṅkhāraṃ assasissāmīti - pe - passasissāmīti sikkhatīti oḷārikaṃ kāyasaṅkhāraṃ passambhento paṭippassambhento nirodhento vūpasamento assasissāmi passasissāmīti sikkhati.
rows.english: 175.(iv) He trains thus: “I shall breathe in … shall breathe out tranquilizing the bodily formation;” he trains thus: “I shall breathe in, shall breathe out tranquilizing, completely tranquilizing, stopping, stilling, the gross bodily formation50”.

rows.pali: Tatra evaṃ oḷārikasukhumatā ca passaddhi ca veditabbā.
rows.english: 176. And here both the gross and subtle state and also [progressive] tranquilizing should be understood.

rows.pali: Imassa hi bhikkhuno pubbe apariggahitakāle kāyo ca cittañca sadarathā honti oḷārikā.
rows.english: For previously, at the time when the bhikkhu has still not discerned [the meditation subject], his body and his mind are disturbed and so they are gross.

rows.pali: Kāyacittānaṃ oḷārikatte avūpasante assāsapassāsāpi oḷārikā honti, balavatarā hutvā pavattanti, nāsikā nappahoti, mukhena assasantopi passasantopi tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: And while the grossness of the body and the mind has still not subsided the in-breaths and out-breaths are gross. They get stronger; his nostrils become inadequate, and he keeps breathing in and out through his mouth.

rows.pali: Yadā panassa kāyopi cittampi pariggahitā honti, tadā te santā honti vūpasantā.
rows.english: But they become quiet and still when his body and mind have been discerned.

rows.pali: Tesu vūpasantesu assāsapassāsā sukhumā hutvā pavattanti, "atthi nu kho natthī"ti vicetabbatākārappattā honti.
rows.english: When they are still then the in-breaths and out-breaths occur so subtly that he has to investigate whether they exist or not.

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi purisassa dhāvitvā, pabbatā vā orohitvā, mahābhāraṃ vā sīsato oropetvā ṭhitassa oḷārikā assāsapassāsā honti, nāsikā nappahoti, mukhena assasantopi passasantopi tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: 177. Suppose a man stands still after running, or descending from a hill, or putting down a big load from his head, then his in-breaths and out-breaths are gross, his nostrils become inadequate, and he keeps on breathing in and out through his mouth.

rows.pali: Yadā panesa taṃ parissamaṃ vinodetvā nhatvā ca pivitvā ca allasāṭakaṃ hadaye katvā sītāya chāyāya nipanno hoti, athassa te assāsapassāsā sukhumā honti "atthi nu kho natthī"ti vicetabbatākārappattā, evameva imassa bhikkhuno pubbe apariggahitakāle kāyo ca - pe - vicetabbatākārappattā honti.
rows.english: But when he has rid himself of his fatigue and has bathed and drunk [275] and put a wet cloth on his heart, and is lying in the cool shade, then his in-breaths and out-breaths eventually occur so subtly that he has to investigate whether they exist or not; so too, previously, at the time when the bhikkhu has still not discerned, … he has to investigate whether they exist or not.

rows.pali: Taṃ kissa hetu?
rows.english: 178. Why is that?

rows.pali: Tathā hissa pubbe apariggahitakāle "oḷārikoḷārike kāyasaṅkhāre passambhemī"ti ābhogasamannāhāramanasikārapaccavekkhaṇā natthi, pariggahitakāle pana atthi.
rows.english: Because previously, at the time when he has still not discerned, there is no concern in him, no reaction, no attention, no reviewing, to the effect that “I am [progressively] tranquilizing each grosser bodily formation. ” But when he has discerned, there is.

rows.pali: Tenassa apariggahitakālato pariggahitakāle kāyasaṅkhāro sukhumo hoti.
rows.english: So his bodily formation at the time when he has discerned is subtle in comparison with that at the time when he has not.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: 221.Pariggahepi oḷāriko, paṭhamajjhānupacāre sukhumo.
rows.english: 179.In discerning [the meditation subject the formation] is gross, and it is subtle [by comparison] in the first-jhāna access;

rows.pali: Tasmimpi oḷāriko, paṭhamajjhāne sukhumo.
rows.english: also it is gross in that, and subtle [by comparison] in the first jhāna;

rows.pali: Paṭhamajjhāne ca dutiyajjhānupacāre ca oḷāriko, dutiyajjhāne sukhumo.
rows.english: in the first jhāna and second-jhāna access it is gross, and in the second jhāna subtle;

rows.pali: Dutiyajjhāne ca tatiyajjhānupacāre ca oḷāriko, tatiyajjhāne sukhumo.
rows.english: in the second jhāna and third-jhāna access it is gross, and in the third jhāna subtle;

rows.pali: Tatiyajjhāne ca catutthajjhānupacāre ca oḷāriko, catutthajjhāne atisukhumo appavattimeva pāpuṇātīti.
rows.english: in the third jhāna and fourth-jhāna access it is gross, and in the fourth jhāna it is so exceedingly subtle that it even reaches cessation.

rows.pali: Idaṃ tāva dīghabhāṇakasaṃyuttabhāṇakānaṃ mataṃ.
rows.english: This is the opinion of the Dīgha and Saṃyutta reciters.

rows.pali: Majjhimabhāṇakā pana paṭhamajjhāne oḷāriko, dutiyajjhānupacāre sukhumoti evaṃ heṭṭhimaheṭṭhimajjhānato uparūparijjhānupacārepi sukhumataramicchanti.
rows.english: But the Majjhima reciters have it that it is subtler in each access than in the jhāna below too in this way: In the first jhāna it is gross, and in the second-jhāna access it is subtle [by comparison, and so on].

rows.pali: Sabbesaññeva pana matena apariggahitakāle pavattakāyasaṅkhāro pariggahitakāle paṭippassambhati.
rows.english: It is, however, the opinion of all that the bodily formation occurring before the time of discerning becomes tranquilized at the time of discerning,

rows.pali: Pariggahitakāle pavattakāyasaṅkhāro paṭhamajjhānupacāre - pe - catutthajjhānupacāre pavattakāyasaṅkhāro catutthajjhāne paṭippassambhati.
rows.english: and the bodily formation at the time of discerning becomes tranquilized in the first-jhāna access … and the bodily formation occurring in the fourth-jhāna access becomes tranquilized in the fourth jhāna.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ tāva samathe nayo.
rows.english: This is the method of explanation in the case of serenity.

rows.pali: Vipassanāyaṃ pana apariggahe pavatto kāyasaṅkhāro oḷāriko, mahābhūtapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: 180. But in the case of insight, the bodily formation occurring at the time of not discerning is gross, and in discerning the primary elements it is [by comparison] subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, upādārūpapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in discerning derived materiality it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, sakalarūpapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in discerning all materiality it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, arūpapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in discerning the immaterial it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, rūpārūpapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in discerning the material and immaterial it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, paccayapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in discerning conditions it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, sappaccayanāmarūpapariggahe sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in seeing mentality-materiality with its conditions it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi oḷāriko, lakkhaṇārammaṇikavipassanāya sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross, and in insight that has the characteristics [of impermanence, etc.,] as its object it is subtle;

rows.pali: Sopi dubbalavipassanāya oḷāriko, balavavipassanāya sukhumo.
rows.english: that also is gross in weak insight while in strong insight it is subtle.

rows.pali: Tattha pubbe vuttanayeneva purimassa purimassa pacchimena pacchimena paṭippassaddhi veditabbā.
rows.english: Herein, the tranquilizing should be understood as [the relative tranquillity] of the subsequent compared with the previous.

rows.pali: Evamettha oḷārikasukhumatā ca passaddhi ca veditabbā.
rows.english: Thus should the gross and subtle state, and the [progressive] tranquilizing, be understood here. [276]

rows.pali: Paṭisambhidāyaṃ (paṭi. ma. 1.171) panassa saddhiṃ codanāsodhanāhi evamattho vutto –
rows.english: 181. But the meaning of this is given in the Paṭisambhidā together with the objection and clarification thus:

rows.pali: Ayaṃ tāvettha kāyānupassanāvasena vuttassa paṭhamacatukkassa anupubbapadavaṇṇanā.
rows.english: This, in the first place, is the consecutive word commentary here on the first tetrad, which deals with contemplation of the body.

rows.pali: 222.Yasmā panettha idameva catukkaṃ ādikammikassa kammaṭṭhānavasena vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 186. The first tetrad is set forth as a meditation subject for a beginner;52

rows.pali: Itarāni pana tīṇi catukkāni ettha pattajjhānassa vedanācittadhammānupassanāvasena vuttāni.
rows.english: but the other three tetrads are [respectively] set forth as the contemplations of feeling, of [the manner of] consciousness, and of mental objects, for one who has already attained jhāna in this tetrad.

rows.pali: Tasmā idaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ bhāvetvā ānāpānacatutthajjhānapadaṭṭhānāya vipassanāya saha paṭisambhidāhi arahattaṃ pāpuṇitukāmena ādikammikena kulaputtena pubbe vuttanayeneva sīlaparisodhanādīni sabbakiccāni katvā vuttappakārassa ācariyassa santike pañcasandhikaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ uggahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So if a clansman who is a beginner wants to develop this meditation subject, and through insight based on the fourth jhāna produced in breathing, to reach Arahantship together with the discriminations, he should first do all the work connected with the purification of virtue, etc., in the way already described, after which he should learn the meditation subject in five stages from a teacher of the kind already described.

rows.pali: Tatrime pañca sandhayo uggaho, paripucchā, upaṭṭhānaṃ, appanā, lakkhaṇanti.
rows.english: 187. Here are the five stages: learning, questioning, establishing, absorption, characteristic.

rows.pali: Tattha uggaho nāma kammaṭṭhānassa uggaṇhanaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, learning is learning the meditation subject.

rows.pali: Paripucchā nāma kammaṭṭhānassa paripucchanā.
rows.english: Questioning is questioning about the meditation subject.

rows.pali: Upaṭṭhānaṃ nāma kammaṭṭhānassa upaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Establishing is establishing the meditation subject.

rows.pali: Appanā nāma kammaṭṭhānassa appanā.
rows.english: Absorption [278] is the absorption of the meditation subject.

rows.pali: Lakkhaṇaṃ nāma kammaṭṭhānassa lakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Characteristic is the characteristic of the meditation subject;

rows.pali: "Evaṃlakkhaṇamidaṃ kammaṭṭhāna"nti kammaṭṭhānasabhāvūpadhāraṇanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: what is meant is that it is the ascertaining of the meditation subject’s individual essence thus: “This meditation subject has such a characteristic. ”

rows.pali: Evaṃ pañcasandhikaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ uggaṇhanto attanāpi na kilamati, ācariyampi na viheseti.
rows.english: 188. Learning the meditation subject in the five stages in this way, he neither tires himself nor worries the teacher.

rows.pali: Tasmā thokaṃ uddisāpetvā bahukālaṃ sajjhāyitvā evaṃ pañcasandhikaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ uggahetvā ācariyassa santike vā aññatra vā pubbe vuttappakāre senāsane vasantena upacchinnakhuddakapalibodhena katabhattakiccena bhattasammadaṃ paṭivinodetvā sukhanisinnena ratanattayaguṇānussaraṇena cittaṃ sampahaṃsetvā ācariyuggahato ekapadampi asammuyhantena idaṃ ānāpānassatikammaṭṭhānaṃ manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: So in giving this meditation subject consisting in mindfulness of breathing attention, he can live either with the teacher or elsewhere in an abode of the kind already described, learning the meditation subject in the five stages thus, getting a little expounded at a time and taking a long time over reciting it. He should sever the minor impediments. After finishing the work connected with the meal and getting rid of any dizziness due to the meal, he should seat himself comfortably. Then, making sure he is not confused about even a single word of what he has learned from the teacher, he should cheer his mind by recollecting the special qualities of the Three Jewels.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ manasikāravidhi –
rows.english: 189. Here are the stages in giving attention to it:

rows.pali: Tattha gaṇanāti gaṇanāyeva.
rows.english: Herein, counting is just counting,

rows.pali: Anubandhanāti anuvahanā.
rows.english: connection is carrying on,

rows.pali: Phusanāti phuṭṭhaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: touching is the place touched [by the breaths],

rows.pali: Ṭhapanāti appanā.
rows.english: fixing is absorption,

rows.pali: Sallakkhaṇāti vipassanā.
rows.english: observing is insight,

rows.pali: Vivaṭṭanāti maggo.
rows.english: turning away is the path,

rows.pali: Pārisuddhīti phalaṃ.
rows.english: purification is fruition,

rows.pali: Tesañca paṭipassanāti paccavekkhaṇā.
rows.english: looking back on these is reviewing.

rows.pali: Tattha iminā ādikammikena kulaputtena paṭhamaṃ gaṇanāya idaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 190. 1. Herein, this clansman who is a beginner should first give attention to this meditation subject by counting.

rows.pali: Gaṇentena ca pañcannaṃ heṭṭhā na ṭhapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: And when counting, he should not stop short of five

rows.pali: Dasannaṃ upari na netabbaṃ.
rows.english: or go beyond ten

rows.pali: Antarā khaṇḍaṃ na dassetabbaṃ.
rows.english: or make any break in the series.

rows.pali: Pañcannaṃ heṭṭhā ṭhapentassa hi sambādhe okāse cittuppādo vipphandati sambādhe vaje sanniruddhagogaṇo viya.
rows.english: By stopping short of five his thoughts get excited in the cramped space, like a herd of cattle shut in a cramped pen.

rows.pali: Dasannampi upari nentassa gaṇananissitako cittuppādo hoti.
rows.english: By going beyond ten his thoughts take the number [rather than the breaths] for their support.

rows.pali: Antarā khaṇḍaṃ dassentassa "sikhāppattaṃ nu kho me kammaṭṭhānaṃ, no"ti cittaṃ vikampati.
rows.english: By making a break in the series he wonders if the meditation subject has reached completion or not.

rows.pali: Tasmā ete dose vajjetvā gaṇetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So he should do his counting without those faults.

rows.pali: Gaṇentena ca paṭhamaṃ dandhagaṇanāya dhaññamāpakagaṇanāya gaṇetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 191. When counting, he should at first do it slowly [that is, late] as a grain measurer does.

rows.pali: Dhaññamāpako hi nāḷiṃ pūretvā "eka"nti vatvā okirati.
rows.english: For a grain measurer, having filled his measure, says “One,” and empties it,

rows.pali: Puna pūrento kiñci kacavaraṃ disvā taṃ chaḍḍento "ekaṃ eka"nti vadati.
rows.english: and then refilling it, he goes on saying ‘”One, one” while removing any rubbish he may have noticed.

rows.pali: Esa nayo dve dvetiādīsu.
rows.english: And the same with “Two, two” and so on.

rows.pali: Evameva imināpi assāsapassāsesu yo upaṭṭhāti, taṃ gahetvā "ekaṃ eka"nti ādiṃ katvā yāva "dasa dasā"ti pavattamānaṃ pavattamānaṃ upalakkhetvāva gaṇetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So, taking the in-breath or the out-breath, whichever appears [most plainly], he should begin with “One, one” [279] and count up to “Ten, ten,” noting each as it occurs.

rows.pali: Tassa evaṃ gaṇayato nikkhamantā ca pavisantā ca assāsapassāsā pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: 192. As he does his counting in this way, the in-breaths and out-breaths become evident to him as they enter in and issue out.

rows.pali: Athānena taṃ dandhagaṇanaṃ dhaññamāpakagaṇanaṃ pahāya sīghagaṇanāya gopālakagaṇanāya gaṇetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Then he can leave off counting slowly (late), like a grain measurer, and he can count quickly [that is, early] as a cowherd does.

rows.pali: Cheko hi gopālako sakkharādayo ucchaṅgena gahetvā rajjudaṇḍahattho pātova vajaṃ gantvā gāvo piṭṭhiyaṃ paharitvā palighatthambhamatthake nisinno dvārappattaṃ dvārappattaṃyeva gāviṃ ekā dveti sakkharaṃ khipitvā gaṇeti.
rows.english: For a skilled cowherd takes pebbles in his pocket and goes to the cow pen in the morning, whip in hand; sitting on the bar of the gate, prodding the cows in the back, he counts each one as it reaches the gate, saying “One, two,” dropping a pebble for each.

rows.pali: Tiyāmarattiṃ sambādhe okāse dukkhaṃ vutthagogaṇo nikkhamanto nikkhamanto aññamaññaṃ upanighaṃsanto vegena vegena puñjapuñjo hutvā nikkhamati.
rows.english: And the cows of the herd, which have been spending the three watches of the night uncomfortably in the cramped space, come out quickly in parties, jostling each other as they escape.

rows.pali: So vegena vegena "tīṇi cattāri pañca dasā"ti gaṇetiyeva, evamimassāpi purimanayena gaṇayato assāsapassāsā pākaṭā hutvā sīghaṃ sīghaṃ punappunaṃ sañcaranti.
rows.english: So he counts quickly (early) “Three, four, five” and so up to ten. In this way the in-breaths and out-breaths, which had already become evident to him while he counted them in the former way, now keep moving along quickly.

rows.pali: Tatonena "punappunaṃ sañcarantī"ti ñatvā anto ca bahi ca agahetvā dvārappattaṃ dvārappattaṃyeva gahetvā "eko dve tīṇi cattāri pañca cha.
rows.english: 193. Then, knowing that they keep moving along quickly, not apprehending them either inside or outside [the body], but apprehending them just as they reach the [nostril] door,

rows.pali: Eko dve tīṇi cattāri pañca cha satta - pe - aṭṭha, nava, dasā"ti sīghaṃ sīyaṃ gaṇetabbameva.
rows.english: he can do his counting quickly (early): “One, two, three, four, five; one, two, three, four, five, six … seven … eight … nine … ten.”

rows.pali: Gaṇanapaṭibaddhe hi kammaṭṭhāne gaṇanabaleneva cittaṃ ekaggaṃ hoti, arittupatthambhanavasena caṇḍasote nāvāṭṭhapanamiva.
rows.english: For as long as the meditation subject is connected with counting it is with the help of that very counting that the mind becomes unified, just as a boat in a swift current is steadied with the help of a rudder.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ sīghaṃ sīghaṃ gaṇayato kammaṭṭhānaṃ nirantaraṃ pavattaṃ viya hutvā upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: 194. When he counts quickly, the meditation subject becomes apparent to him as an uninterrupted process.

rows.pali: Atha nirantaraṃ pavattatīti ñatvā anto ca bahi ca vātaṃ apariggahetvā purimanayeneva vegena vegena gaṇetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Then, knowing that it is proceeding uninterruptedly, he can count quickly (early) in the way just described, not discerning the wind either inside or outside [the body].

rows.pali: Anto pavisanavātena hi saddhiṃ cittaṃ pavesayato abbhantaraṃ vātabbhāhataṃ medapūritaṃ viya hoti.
rows.english: For by bringing his consciousness inside along with the incoming breath, it seems as if it were buffeted by the wind inside or filled with fat.53

rows.pali: Bahi nikkhamanavātena saddhiṃ cittaṃ nīharato bahiddhā puthuttārammaṇe cittaṃ vikkhipati.
rows.english: By taking his consciousness outside along with the outgoing breath, it gets distracted by the multiplicity of objects outside.

rows.pali: Phuṭṭhaphuṭṭhokāse pana satiṃ ṭhapetvā bhāventasseva bhāvanā sampajjati.
rows.english: However, his development is successful when he fixes his mindfulness on the place touched [by the breaths].

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "anto ca bahi ca vātaṃ apariggahetvā purimanayeneva vegena vegena gaṇetabba"nti.
rows.english: That is why it was said above: “He can count quickly (early) in the way just described, not discerning the wind either inside or outside. ”

rows.pali: Kīvaciraṃ panetaṃ gaṇetabbanti?
rows.english: 195. But how long is he to go on counting?

rows.pali: Yāva vinā gaṇanāya assāsapassāsārammaṇe sati santiṭṭhati.
rows.english: Until, without counting, [280] mindfulness remains settled on the in-breaths and out-breaths as its object.

rows.pali: Bahivisaṭavitakkavicchedaṃ katvā assāsapassāsārammaṇe satisaṇṭhāpanatthaṃyeva hi gaṇanāti.
rows.english: For counting is simply a device for setting mindfulness on the in-breaths and out- breaths as object by cutting off the external dissipation of applied thoughts.

rows.pali: 224.Evaṃ gaṇanāya manasi katvā anubandhanāya manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 196. 2. Having given attention to it in this way by counting, he should now do so by connection.

rows.pali: Anubandhanā nāma gaṇanaṃ paṭisaṃharitvā satiyā nirantaraṃ assāsapassāsānaṃ anugamanaṃ.
rows.english: Connection is the uninterrupted following of the in-breaths and out-breaths with mindfulness after counting has been given up.

rows.pali: Tañca kho na ādimajjhapariyosānānugamanavasena.
rows.english: And that is not by following after the beginning, the middle and the end. 54

rows.pali: Bahinikkhamanavātassa hi nābhi ādi, hadayaṃ majjhaṃ, nāsikaggaṃ pariyosānaṃ.
rows.english: 197. The navel is the beginning of the wind issuing out, the heart is its middle and the nose-tip is its end.

rows.pali: Abbhantaraṃ pavisanavātassa nāsikaggaṃ ādi, hadayaṃ majjhaṃ nābhi pariyosānaṃ.
rows.english: The nose-tip is the beginning of the wind entering in, the heart is its middle and the navel is its end.

rows.pali: Tañcassa anugacchato vikkhepagataṃ cittaṃ sāraddhāya ceva hoti iñjanāya ca.
rows.english: And if he follows after that, his mind is distracted by disquiet and perturbation

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Tasmā anubandhanāya manasikarontena ādimajjhapariyosānavasena na manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 3–4. So when he gives his attention to it by connection, he should do so not by the beginning, middle and end,

rows.pali: Apica kho phusanāvasena ca ṭhapanāvasena ca manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: but rather by touching and by fixing.

rows.pali: Gaṇanānubandhanāvasena viya hi phusanāṭhapanāvasena visuṃ manasikāro natthi.
rows.english: 198. There is no attention to be given to it by touching separate from fixing as there is by counting separate from connection.

rows.pali: Phuṭṭhaphuṭṭhaṭṭhāneyeva pana gaṇento gaṇanāya ca phusanāya ca manasi karoti.
rows.english: But when he is counting the breaths in the place touched by each, he is giving attention to them by counting and touching.

rows.pali: Tattheva gaṇanaṃ paṭisaṃharitvā te satiyā anubandhanto, appanāvasena ca cittaṃ ṭhapento anubandhanāya ca phusanāya ca ṭhapanāya ca manasi karotīti vuccati.
rows.english: When he has given up counting and is connecting them by means of mindfulness in that same place and fixing consciousness by means of absorption, then he is said to be giving his attention to them by connection, touching and fixing.

rows.pali: Svāyamattho aṭṭhakathāsu vuttapaṅguḷadovārikūpamāhi paṭisambhidāyaṃ vuttakakacūpamāya ca veditabbo.
rows.english: And the meaning of this may be understood through the similes of the man who cannot walk and the gatekeeper given in the commentaries, and through the simile of the saw given in the Paṭisambhidā.

rows.pali: 225.Tatrāyaṃ paṅguḷopamā – seyyathāpi paṅguḷo dolāya kīḷataṃ mātāputtānaṃ dolaṃ khipitvā tattheva dolāthambhamūle nisinno kamena āgacchantassa ca gacchantassa ca dolāphalakassa ubho koṭiyo majjhañca passati, na ca ubhokoṭimajjhānaṃ dassanatthaṃ byāvaṭo hoti, evamevāyaṃ bhikkhu sativasena upanibandhanathambhamūle ṭhatvā assāsapassāsadolaṃ khipitvā tattheva nimitte satiyā nisīdanto kamena āgacchantānañca gacchantānañca phuṭṭhaṭṭhāne assāsapassāsānaṃ ādimajjhapariyosānaṃ satiyā anugacchanto tattha ca cittaṃ ṭhapento passati, na ca tesaṃ dassanatthaṃ byāvaṭo hoti, ayaṃ paṅguḷopamā.
rows.english: 199. Here is the simile of the man who cannot walk: Just as a man unable to walk, who is rocking a swing for the amusement of his children and their mother, sits at the foot of the swing post and sees both ends and the middle of the swing plank successively coming and going, [281] yet does not move from his place in order to see both ends and the middle, so too, when a bhikkhu places himself with mindfulness, as it were, at the foot of the post for anchoring [mindfulness] and rocks the swing of the in-breaths and out-breaths; he sits down with mindfulness on the sign at that same place, and follows with mindfulness the beginning, middle and end of the in-breaths and out-breaths at the place touched by them as they come and go; keeping his mind fixed there, he then sees them without moving from his place in order to see them. This is the simile of the man who cannot walk.

rows.pali: 226.Ayaṃ pana dovārikūpamā – seyyathāpi dovāriko nagarassa anto ca bahi ca purise "ko tvaṃ, kuto vā āgato, kuhiṃ vā gacchasi, kiṃ vā te hatthe"ti na vīmaṃsati.
rows.english: 200. This is the simile of the gatekeeper: Just as a gatekeeper does not examine people inside and outside the town, asking, “Who are you? Where have you come from? Where are you going? What have you got in your hand?”

rows.pali: Na hi tassa te bhārā, dvārappattaṃ dvārappattaṃyeva pana vīmaṃsati, evameva imassa bhikkhuno antopaviṭṭhavātā ca bahinikkhantavātā ca na bhārā honti, dvārappattā dvārappattāyeva bhārāti ayaṃ dovārikūpamā.
rows.english: —for those people are not his concern—but he does examine each man as he arrives at the gate, so too, the incoming breaths that have gone inside and the outgoing breaths that have gone outside are not this bhikkhu’s concern, but they are his concern each time they arrive at the [nostril] gate itself.

rows.pali: 227.Kakacūpamā pana ādito paṭṭhāya evaṃ veditabbā.
rows.english: 201. Then the simile of the saw should be understood from its beginning.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ –
rows.english: For this is said:

rows.pali: Ayaṃ kakacūpamā.
rows.english: This is the simile of the saw.

rows.pali: Idha panassa āgatāgatavasena amanasikāramattameva payojananti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: But here it is precisely his not giving attention [to the breaths] as [yet to] come and [already] gone56 that should be understood as the purpose.

rows.pali: 228.Idaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ manasikaroto kassaci na cireneva nimittañca uppajjati, avasesajhānaṅgapaṭimaṇḍitā appanāsaṅkhātā ṭhapanā ca sampajjati.
rows.english: 204. When someone gives his attention to this meditation subject, sometimes it is not long before the sign arises in him, and then the fixing, in other words, absorption adorned with the rest of the jhāna factors, is achieved.

rows.pali: Kassaci pana gaṇanāvaseneva manasikārakālato pabhuti anukkamato oḷārikaassāsapassāsanirodhavasena kāyadarathe vūpasante kāyopi cittampi lahukaṃ hoti, sarīraṃ ākāse laṅghanākārappattaṃ viya hoti.
rows.english: 205. After someone has given his attention to counting, both body and mind become light as though the body would leap up into the air.

rows.pali: Yathā sāraddhakāyassa mañce vā pīṭhe vā nisīdato mañcapīṭhaṃ onamati, vikūjati, paccattharaṇaṃ valiṃ gaṇhāti.
rows.english: Just as when a body that is disturbed sits down on a bed or chair, the bed or chair sags down and creaks and the cover gets rumpled,

rows.pali: Asāraddhakāyassa pana nisīdato neva mañcapīṭhaṃ onamati, na vikūjati, na paccattharaṇaṃ valiṃ gaṇhāti, tūlapicupūritaṃ viya mañcapīṭhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: but when a body that is not disturbed sits down, the bed or chair neither sags down nor creaks, the cover does not get rumpled, and it is as though filled with cotton wool—

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: why?

rows.pali: Yasmā asāraddho kāyo lahuko hoti.
rows.english: because a body that is not disturbed is light—

rows.pali: Evameva gaṇanāvasena manasikārakālato pabhuti anukkamato oḷārikaassāsapassāsanirodhavasena kāyadarathe vūpasante kāyopi cittampi lahukaṃ hoti, sarīraṃ ākāse laṅghanākārappattaṃ viya hoti.
rows.english: so too, after he has given his attention to counting, when the bodily disturbance has been stilled by the gradual cessation of gross in-breaths and out-breaths, then both the body and the mind become light: the physical body is as though it were ready to leap up into the air. [283]
